title: Tackling the Generative Learning Trilemma with Denoising Diffusion GANs

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## TACKLING THE GENERATIVE LEARNING TRILEMMA WITH DENOISING DIFFUSION GANS

**Zhisheng Xiao** _[∗]_ The University of Chicago zxiao@uchicago.edu

**Karsten Kreis Arash Vahdat** NVIDIA NVIDIA kkreis@nvidia.com avahdat@nvidia.com

## ABSTRACT

A wide variety of deep generative models has been developed in the past decade. Yet, these models often struggle with simultaneously addressing three key requirements including: high sample quality, mode coverage, and fast sampling. We call the challenge imposed by these requirements _the generative learning trilemma_ , as the existing models often trade some of them for others. Particularly, denoising diffusion models have shown impressive sample quality and diversity, but their expensive sampling does not yet allow them to be applied in many real-world applications. In this paper, we argue that slow sampling in these models is fundamentally attributed to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step which is justified only for small step sizes. To enable denoising with large steps, and hence, to reduce the total number of denoising steps, we propose to model the denoising distribution using a complex multimodal distribution. We introduce _denoising diffusion generative adversarial networks (denoising diffusion GANs)_ that model each denoising step using a multimodal conditional GAN. Through extensive evaluations, we show that denoising diffusion GANs obtain sample quality and diversity competitive with original diffusion models while being 2000 _×_ faster on the CIFAR-10 dataset. Compared to traditional GANs, our model exhibits better mode coverage and sample diversity. To the best of our knowledge, denoising diffusion GAN is the first model that reduces sampling cost in diffusion models to an extent that allows them to be applied to real-world applications inexpensively. Project page and code: https://nvlabs.github.io/denoising-diffusion-gan.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In the past decade, a plethora of deep generative models has been developed for various domains such as images (Karras et al., 2019; Razavi et al., 2019), audio (Oord et al., 2016a; Kong et al., 2021), point clouds (Yang et al., 2019) and graphs (De Cao & Kipf, 2018). However, current generative learning frameworks cannot yet simultaneously satisfy three key requirements, often needed for their wide adoption in real-world problems. These requirements include (i) high-quality sampling, (ii) mode coverage and sample diversity, and (iii) fast and computationally inexpensive sampling. For example, most current works in image synthesis focus on high-quality generation. However, mode coverage and data

Additionally, applications such as interactive image editing or real-time speech synthesis require fast sampling. Here, we identify the challenge posed by these requirements as the _generative learning trilemma_ , since existing models usually compromise between them.

Fig. 1 summarizes how mainstream generative frameworks tackle the trilemma. Generative adversarial networks (GANs) (Goodfellow et al., 2014; Brock et al., 2018) generate high-quality samples rapidly, but they have poor mode coverage (Salimans et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2018). Conversely,

variational autoencoders (VAEs) (Kingma & Welling, 2014; Rezende et al., 2014) and normalizing flows (Dinh et al., 2016; Kingma & Dhariwal, 2018) cover data modes faithfully, but they often suffer from low sample quality. Recently, diffusion models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021c) have emerged as powerful generative models. They demonstrate surprisingly good results in sample quality, beating GANs in image generation (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021; Ho et al., 2021). They also obtain good mode coverage, indicated by high likelihood (Song et al., 2021b; Kingma et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021). Although diffusion models have been applied to a variety of tasks (Dhariwal & Nichol; Austin et al.; Mittal et al.; Luo & Hu), sampling from them often requires thousands of network evaluations, making their application expensive in practice.

In this paper, we tackle the generative learning trilemma by reformulating denoising diffusion models specifically for fast sampling while maintaining strong mode coverage and sample quality. We investigate the slow sampling issue of diffusion models and we observe that diffusion models commonly assume that the denoising distribution can be approximated by Gaussian distributions. However, it is known that the Gaussian assumption holds only in the infinitesimal limit of small denoising steps (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Feller, 1949), which leads to the requirement of a large number of steps in the reverse process. When the reverse process uses larger step sizes (i.e., it has fewer denoising steps), we need a non-Gaussian multimodal distribution for modeling the denoising distribution. Intuitively, in image synthesis, the multimodal distribution arises from the fact that multiple plausible clean images may correspond to the same noisy image.

Inspired by this observation, we propose to parametrize the denoising distribution with an expressive multimodal distribution to enable denoising for large steps. In particular, we introduce a novel generative model, termed as _denoising diffusion GAN_ , in which the denoising distributions are modeled with conditional GANs. In image generation, we observe that our model obtains sample quality and mode coverage competitive with diffusion models, while taking only as few as two denoising steps, achieving about 2000 _×_ speed-up in sampling compared to the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) on CIFAR-10. Compared to traditional GANs, we show that our model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art GANs in sample diversity, while being competitive in sample fidelity.

In summary, we make the following contributions: i) We attribute the slow sampling of diffusion models to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising distribution and propose to employ complex, multimodal denoising distributions. ii) We propose denoising diffusion GANs, a diffusion model whose reverse process is parametrized by conditional GANs. iii) Through careful evaluations, we demonstrate that denoising diffusion GANs achieve several orders of magnitude speed-up compared to current diffusion models for both image generation and editing. We show that our model overcomes the deep generative learning trilemma to a large extent, making diffusion models for the first time applicable to interactive, real-world applications at a low computational cost.

Two key assumptions are commonly made in diffusion models: First, the denoising distribution _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is modeled with a Gaussian distribution. Second, the number of denoising steps _T_ is often assumed to be in the order of hundreds to thousands of steps. In this paper, we focus on discrete-time diffusion models. In continuous-time diffusion models (Song et al., 2021c), similar assumptions are also made at the sampling time when discretizing time into small timesteps.

## 3 DENOISING DIFFUSION GANS

We first discuss why reducing the number of denoising steps requires learning a multimodal denoising distribution in Sec. 3.1. Then, we present our multimodal denoising model in Sec. 3.2.

## 3.1 MULTIMODAL DENOISING DISTRIBUTIONS FOR LARGE DENOISING STEPS

As we discussed in Sec. 2, a common assumption in the diffusion model literature is to approximate _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) with a Gaussian distribution. Here, we question when such an approximation is accurate.

The true denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) can be written as _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) _∝ q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1) using Bayes’ rule where _q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) is the forward Gaussian diffusion shown in Eq. 1 and _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1) is the marginal data distribution at step _t_ . It can be shown that in two situations the true denoising distribution takes a Gaussian form. First, in the limit of infinitesimal step size _βt_ , the product in the Bayes’ rule is dominated by _q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) and the reversal of the diffusion process takes an identical functional form as the forward process (Feller, 1949). Thus, when _βt_ is sufficiently small, since _q_ ( **x** _t|_ **x** _t−_ 1) is a Gaussian, the denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is also Gaussian, and the approximation used by current diffusion models can be accurate. To satisfy this, diffusion models often have thousands of steps with small _βt_ . Second, if data marginal _q_ ( **x** _t_ ) is Gaussian, the denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is also a Gaussian distribution. The idea of bringing data distribution _q_ ( **x** 0) and consequently _q_ ( **x** _t_ ) closer to Gaussian using a VAE encoder was recently explored in LSGM (Vahdat et al., 2021). However, the problem of transforming the data to Gaussian itself is challenging and VAE encoders cannot solve it perfectly. That is why LSGM still requires tens to hundreds of steps on complex datasets.

In this paper, we argue that when neither of the conditions are met, i.e., when the denoising step is large and the data distribution is non-Gaussian, there are no guarantees that the Gaussian assumption on the denoising distribution holds. To illustrate this, in Fig. 2, we visualize the true denoising distribution for different denoising step sizes for a multimodal data distribution. We see that as the denoising step gets larger, the true denoising distribution becomes more complex and multimodal.

## 3.2 MODELING DENOISING DISTRIBUTIONS WITH CONITIONAL GANS

Our goal is to reduce the number of denoising diffusion steps _T_ required in the reverse process of diffusion models. Inspired by the observation above, we propose to model the denoising distribution

Since conditional GANs have been shown to model complex conditional distributions in the image domain (Mirza & Osindero, 2014; Ledig et al., 2017; Isola et al., 2017), we adopt them to approximate the true denoising distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ).

Specifically, our forward diffusion is set up similarly to the diffusion models in Eq. 1 with the main assumption that _T_ is assumed to be small ( _T ≤_ 8) and each diffusion step has larger _βt_ . Our training is formulated by matching the conditional GAN generator _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) and _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) using an adversarial loss that minimizes a divergence _D_ adv per denoising step:

where _D_ adv can be Wasserstein distance, Jenson-Shannon divergence, or f-divergence depending on the adversarial training setup (Arjovsky et al., 2017; Goodfellow et al., 2014; Nowozin et al., 2016). In this paper, we rely on non-saturating GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014), that are widely used in successful GAN frameworks such as StyleGANs (Karras et al., 2019; 2020b). In this case, _D_ adv takes a special instance of f-divergence called _softened reverse KL_ (Shannon et al., 2020), which is different from the forward KL divergence used in the original diffusion model training in Eq. 3[1] .

**Parametrizing the implicit denoising model:** Instead of directly predicting **x** _t−_ 1 in the denoising step, diffusion models (Ho et al., 2020) can be interpreted as parameterizing the denoising model by _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) := _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t,_ **x** 0 = _fθ_ ( **x** _t, t_ )) in which first **x** 0 is predicted using the denoising model _fθ_ ( **x** _t, t_ ), and then, **x** _t−_ 1 is sampled using the posterior distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t,_ **x** 0) given **x** _t_ and the predicted **x** 0 (See Appendix B for details). The distribution _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** 0 _,_ **x** _t_ ) is intuitively the distribution over **x** _t−_ 1 when denoising from **x** _t_ towards **x** 0, and it always has a Gaussian form for the diffusion process in Eq. 1, independent of the step size and complexity of the data distribution (see Appendix A for the expression of _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** 0 _,_ **x** _t_ )). Similarly, we define _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) by:

where _pθ_ ( **x** 0 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is the implicit distribution imposed by the GAN generator _Gθ_ ( **x** _t,_ **z** _, t_ ) : R _[N] ×_ R _[L] ×_ R _→_ R _[N]_ that outputs **x** 0 given **x** _t_ and an _L_ -dimensional latent variable **z** _∼ p_ ( **z** ) := _N_ ( **z** ; **0** _,_ **I** ).

Our parameterization has several advantages: Firstly, our _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) is formulated similar to DDPM (Ho et al., 2020). Thus, we can borrow some inductive bias such as the network structure design from DDPM. The main difference is that, in DDPM, **x** 0 is predicted as a deterministic mapping of **x** _t_ , while in our case **x** 0 is produced by the generator with random latent variable **z** . This is the key difference that allows our denoising distribution _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) to become multimodal and complex in contrast to the unimodal denoising model in DDPM. Secondly, note that for different _t_ ’s, **x** _t_ has different levels of perturbation, and hence using a single network to predict **x** _t−_ 1 directly at different _t_ may be difficult. However, in our case the generator only needs to predict unperturbed **x** 0 and then add back perturbation using _q_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t,_ **x** 0). Fig. 3 visualizes our training pipeline.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Diffusion-based models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020) learn the finite-time reversal of a diffusion process, sharing the idea of learning transition operators of Markov chains with Goyal et al. (2017); Alain et al. (2016); Bordes et al. (2017). Since then, there have been a number of improvements and alternatives to diffusion models. Song et al. (2021c) generalize diffusion processes to continuous time, and provide a unified view of diffusion models and denoising score matching (Vincent, 2011; Song & Ermon, 2019). Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) add an auxiliary adversarial loss to the main objective. This is fundamentally different from ours, as their auxiliary adversarial loss only acts as an image enhancer, and they do not use latent variables; therefore, the denoising distribution is still a unimodal Gaussian. Other explorations include introducing alternative noise distributions in the forward process (Nachmani et al., 2021), jointly optimizing the model and noise schedule (Kingma et al., 2021) and applying the model in latent spaces (Vahdat et al., 2021).

One major drawback of diffusion or score-based models is the slow sampling speed due to a large number of iterative sampling steps. To alleviate this issue, multiple methods have been proposed, including knowledge distillation (Luhman & Luhman, 2021), learning an adaptive noise schedule (San-Roman et al., 2021), introducing non-Markovian diffusion processes (Song et al., 2021a; Kong & Ping, 2021), and using better SDE solvers for continuous-time models (Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2021a). In particular, Song et al. (2021a) uses **x** 0 sampling as a crucial ingredient to their method, but their denoising distribution is still a Gaussian. These methods either suffer from significant degradation in sample quality, or still require many sampling steps as we demonstrate in Sec. 5.

Among variants of diffusion models, Gao et al. (2021) have the closest connection with our method. They propose to model the single-step denoising distribution by a conditional energy-based model (EBM), sharing the high-level idea of using expressive denoising distributions with us. However, they motivate their method from the perspective of facilitating the training of EBMs. More importantly, although only a few denoising steps are needed, expensive MCMC has to be used to sample from each denoising step, making the sampling process slow with _∼_ 180 network evaluations. ImageBART (Esser et al., 2021a) explores modeling the denoising distribution of a diffusion process on discrete latent space with an auto-regressive model per step in a few denoising steps. However, the auto-regressive structure of their denoising distribution still makes sampling slow.

Since our model is trained with adversarial loss, our work is related to recent advances in improving the sample quality and diversity of GANs, including data augmentation (Zhao et al., 2020; Karras et al., 2020a), consistency regularization (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021) and entropy regularization (Dieng et al., 2019). In addition, the idea of training generative models with smoothed distributions is also discussed in Meng et al. (2021a) for auto-regressive models.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we evaluate our proposed denoising diffusion GAN for the image synthesis problem. We begin with briefly introducing the network architecture design, while additional implementation details are presented in Appendix C. For our GAN generator, we adopt the NCSN++ architecture from Song et al. (2021c) which has a U-net structure (Ronneberger et al., 2015). The conditioning **x** _t_ is the input of the network, and time embedding is used to ensure conditioning on _t_ . We let the latent variable **z** control the normalization layers. In particular, we replace all group normalization layers (Wu & He, 2018) in NCSN++ with adaptive group normalization layers in the generator, similar to Karras et al. (2019); Huang & Belongie (2017), where the shift and scale parameters in group normalization are predicted from **z** using a simple multi-layer fully-connected network.

## 5.1 OVERCOMING THE GENERATIVE LEARNING TRILEMMA

One major highlight of our model is that it excels at all three criteria in the generative learning trilemma. Here, we carefully evaluate our model’s performances on sample fidelity, sample diversity and sampling time, and benchmark against a comprehensive list of models on the CIFAR-10 dataset.

**Evaluation criteria:** We adopt the commonly used Fr´echet inception distance (FID) (Heusel et al., 2017) and Inception Score (IS) (Salimans et al., 2016) for evaluating sample fidelity. We use the training set as a reference to compute the FID, following common practice in the literature (see Ho et al. (2020); Karras et al. (2019) as an example). For sample diversity, we use the improved recall score from Kynk¨a¨anniemi et al. (2019), which is an improved version of the original precision and recall metric proposed by Sajjadi et al. (2018). It is shown that an improved recall score reflects how the variation in the generated samples matches that in the training set (Kynk¨a¨anniemi et al., 2019). For sampling time, we use the number of function evaluations (NFE) and the clock time when generating a batch of 100 images on a V100 GPU.

**Results:** We present our quantitative results in Table 1. We observe that our sample quality is competitive among the best diffusion models and GANs. Although some variants of diffusion models obtain better IS and FID, they require a large number of function evaluations to generate samples (while we use only 4 denoising steps). For example, our sampling time is about 2000 _×_ faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and _∼_ 20 _×_ faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021). Note that diffusion models can produce samples in fewer steps while trading off the sample quality. To better benchmark our method against existing diffusion models, we plot the FID score versus sampling time of diffusion models by varying the number of denoising steps (or the error tolerance for continuous-time models) in Figure 4. The figure clearly shows the advantage of our model compared to previous diffusion models. When comparing our model to GANs, we observe that only StyleGAN2 with adaptive data augmentation has slightly better sample quality than ours. However, from Table 1, we see that GANs have limited sample diversity, as their recall scores are below 0 _._ 5. In contrast, our model obtains a significantly better recall score, even higher than several advanced likelihood-based models, and competitive among diffusion models. We show qualitative samples of CIFAR-10 in Figure 5. In summary, our model simultaneously excels at sample quality, sample diversity, and sampling speed and tackles the generative learning trilemma by a large extent.

## 5.2 ABLATION STUDIES

Here, we provide additional insights into our model by performing ablation studies.

**Number of denoising steps:** In the first part of Table 2, we study the effect of using a different number of denoising steps ( _T_ ). Note that _T_ = 1 corresponds to training an unconditional GAN, as the conditioning **x** _t_ contains almost no information about **x** 0. We observe that _T_ = 1 leads to significantly worse results with low sample diversity, indicated by the low recall score. This confirms the benefits of breaking generation into several denoising steps, especially for improving the sample diversity. When varying _T >_ 1, we observe that _T_ =4 gives the best results, whereas there is a slight degrade in performance for larger _T_ . We hypothesize that we may require a significantly higher capacity to accommodate larger _T_ , as we need a conditional GAN for each denoising step. **Diffusion as data augmentation:** Our model shares some similarities with recent work on applying data augmentation to GANs (Karras et al., 2020a; Zhao et al., 2020). To study the effect of perturbing inputs, we train a one-shot GAN with our network structure following the protocol in (Zhao et al., 2020) with the forward diffusion process as data augmentation. The result, presented in the second group of Table 2, is significantly worse than our model, indicating that our model is not equivalent to augmenting data before applying the discriminator.

**Parametrization for** _pθ_ ( **x** _t−_ 1 _|_ **x** _t_ ) **:** We study two alternative ways to parametrize the denoising distribution for the same _T_ = 4 setting. Instead of letting the generator produce estimated samples of **x** 0, we set the generator to directly output denoised samples **x** _t−_ 1 without posterior sampling ( _direct denoising_ ), or output the noise _ϵt_ that perturbs a clean image to produce **x** _t_ ( _noise generation_ ). Note that the latter case is closely related to most diffusion models where the network deterministically predicts the perturbation noise. In Table 2, we show that although these alternative parametrizations work reasonably well, our main parametrization outperforms them by a large margin.

**Importance of latent variable:** Removing latent variables **z** converts our denoising model to a unimodal distribution. In the last line of Table 2, we study our model’s performance without any latent variables **z** . We see that the sample quality is significantly worse, suggesting the importance of multimodal denoising distributions. In Figure 8, we visualize the effect of latent variables by showing samples of _pθ_ ( **x** 0 _|_ **x** 1), where **x** 1 is a fixed noisy observation. We see that while the majority of information in the conditioning **x** 1 is preserved, the samples are diverse due to the latent variables.

## 5.3 ADDITIONAL STUDIES

**Mode Coverage:** Besides the recall score in Table 1, we also evaluate the mode coverage of our model on the popular 25-Gaussians and StackedMNIST. The 25-Gaussians dataset is a 2-D toy dataset, generated by a mixture of 25 two-dimensional Gaussian distributions, arranged in a grid. We train our denoising diffusion GAN with 4 denoising steps and compare it to other models in Figure 6. We observe that the vanilla GAN suffers severely from mode collapse, and while techniques like WGAN-GP (Gulrajani et al., 2017) improve mode coverage, the sample quality is still limited. In contrast, our model covers all the modes while maintaining high sample quality. We also train a diffusion model and plot the samples generated by 100 and 500 denoising steps. We see that diffusion models require a large number of steps to maintain high sample quality.

Real samples GAN WGAN-GP Ours, T=4 DDPM, T=500 DDPM, T=100

Figure 6: Qualitative results on the 25-Gaussians dataset.

StackMNIST contains images generated by randomly choosing 3 MNIST images and stacking them along the RGB channels. Hence, the data distribution has 1000 modes. Following the setting of Lin et al. (2018), we report the number of covered modes and the KL divergence from the categorical distribution over 1000 categories of generated samples to true data in Table 3. We observe that our model covers all modes faithfully and achieves the lowest KL compared to GANs that are specifically designed for better mode coverage or StyleGAN2 that is known to have the best sample quality.

## **Training Stability:** We discuss the training stability of our model in Appendix D.

**High Resolution Images:** We train our model on datasets with larger images, including CelebAHQ (Karras et al., 2018) and LSUN Church (Yu et al., 2015) at 256 _×_ 256px resolution. We report FID on these two datasets in Table 4 and 5. Similar to CIFAR-10, our model obtains competitive sample quality among the best diffusion models and GANs. In particular, in LSUN Church, our model outperforms DDPM and ImageBART (see Figure 7 and Appendix E for samples). Although, some GANs perform better on this dataset, their mode coverage is not reflected by the FID score.

**Stroke-based image synthesis:** Recently, Meng et al. (2021b) propose an interesting application of diffusion models to stroke-based generation. Specifically, they perturb a stroke painting by the forward diffusion process, and denoise it with a diffusion model. The method is particularly promising because it only requires training an unconditional generative model on the target dataset and does not require training images paired with stroke paintings like GAN-based methods (Sangkloy et al., 2017; Park et al., 2019). We apply our model to stroke-based image synthesis and show qualitative results in Figure 9. The generated samples are realistic and diverse, while the conditioning in the stroke paintings is faithfully preserved. Compared to Meng et al. (2021b), our model enjoys a 1100 _×_ speedup in generation, as it takes only **0.16** s to generate one image at 256 resolution vs. **181** s for Meng et al. (2021b). This experiment confirms that our proposed model enables the application of diffusion models to interactive applications such as image editing.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

Deep generative learning frameworks still struggle with addressing the generative learning trilemma. Diffusion models achieve particularly high-quality and diverse sampling. However, their slow sampling and high computational cost do not yet allow them to be widely applied in real-world applications. In this paper, we argued that one of the main sources of slow sampling in diffusion models is the Gaussian assumption in the denoising distribution, which is justified only for very small denoising steps. To remedy this, we proposed denoising diffusion GANs that model each denoising step using a complex multimodal distribution, enabling us to take large denoising steps. In extensive experiments, we showed that denoising diffusion GANs achieve high sample quality and diversity competitive to the original diffusion models, while being orders of magnitude faster at sampling. Compared to traditional GANs, our proposed model enjoys better mode coverage and sample diversity. Our denoising diffusion GAN overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent, allowing diffusion models to be applied to real-world problems with low computational cost.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 7 ETHICS AND REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

Generating high-quality samples while representing the diversity in the training data faithfully has been a daunting challenge in generative learning. Mode coverage and high diversity are key requirements for reducing biases in generative models and improving the representation of minorities in a population. While diffusion models achieve both high sample quality and diversity, their expensive sampling limits their application in many real-world problems. Our proposed denoising diffusion GAN reduces the computational complexity of diffusion models to an extent that allows these models to be applied in practical applications at a low cost. Thus, we foresee that in the long term, our model can help with reducing the negative social impacts of existing generative models that fall short in capturing the data diversity.
We evaluate our model using public datasets, and therefore this work does not involve any human subject evaluation or new data collection.

**Reproducibility Statement:** We currently provide experimental details in the appendices with a detailed list of hyperparameters and training settings. To aid with reproducibility further, we will release our source code publicly in the future with instructions to reproduce our results.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel hybrid architecture—denoising diffusion GANs—that aims to resolve the tension between sample quality, diversity, and sampling speed in generative modeling. It makes a compelling empirical case on CIFAR-10 and other benchmarks, showing strong quantitative performance and visual results. The idea of replacing Gaussian denoising steps with multimodal GAN-based transitions is conceptually interesting and aligns with known limitations of diffusion models in large-step regimes. The ablation studies are informative and support key design choices.

Overview issues:

- Lack of theoretical justification for multimodal denoising distribution matching: The paper asserts that the Gaussian assumption in diffusion models breaks down for large step sizes and proposes using GANs to model complex, multimodal denoising distributions. However, it provides no theoretical analysis of whether the adversarial training objective in Eq. 4 actually recovers the true reverse process or a valid approximation of it. The original diffusion framework relies on variational inference with KL divergence minimization, whereas the proposed method uses adversarial divergence (e.g., JS or Wasserstein), which may not correspond to a consistent estimation of the posterior. Without a theoretical bridge between the two, it's unclear whether the model converges to a distribution that approximates the data-generating process or merely wins the game against the discriminator. This gap undermines confidence in the method's correctness beyond empirical performance.

- Inadequate comparison to state-of-the-art fast diffusion samplers: The paper claims a 2000× speedup over Song et al. (2021c), but this comparison uses a slow predictor-corrector sampler with 2000 steps. More relevant baselines like DDIM (Song et al., 2021a), DPM-Solver (Lu et al., 2022), or progressive distillation (Salimans & Ho, 2022) achieve high-quality samples in 10–50 steps and are directly comparable to the proposed 4-step generation. Table 1 includes DDIM at T=50, but no results are reported for T≤10, which would be a fairer comparison. The absence of such comparisons overstates the speed advantage. The paper should benchmark against these methods at matched step counts or quality levels to demonstrate true progress in the speed-quality trade-off.

- Overreliance on FID and IS without deeper diversity analysis: While the paper reports FID, IS, and recall, it treats them as sufficient indicators of mode coverage. However, high recall on CIFAR-10 does not rule out mode collapse in more structured or semantic dimensions. The StackedMNIST experiment is a strength, but it's isolated and not extended to image datasets with known semantic modes (e.g., CelebA attributes). Moreover, the paper does not analyze whether the diversity comes from meaningful variations or texture-level noise. Given the use of GANs, which can produce high-recall scores through small perturbations, a more rigorous analysis—such as linear separability of modes or human evaluation of semantic diversity—is needed to substantiate the claim of superior mode coverage.

- Unrealistic sampling time comparison due to unreported batch constraints: The reported 0.21s generation time for 100 images on CIFAR-10 (2.1ms per image) appears suspiciously fast for a 4-step GAN-based model with U-Net architecture. In contrast, StyleGAN2 takes 0.04s for a single image. This suggests either an error in timing or that the denoising diffusion GAN benefits from heavy batching or optimized inference not available to other models. The paper does not clarify whether the timing includes data loading, preprocessing, or GAN truncation effects. Without standardized measurement conditions—especially batch size and GPU utilization—the claimed 2000× speedup lacks credibility and could mislead readers about practical applicability.

- Insufficient discussion of training stability and failure modes: The integration of GANs into a multi-step diffusion framework introduces significant training complexity. While the paper mentions training stability is discussed in Appendix D, no details are provided in the main text or appendices (as submitted). Given the known instability of GANs, especially with multiple discriminators per step or complex conditioning, readers cannot assess reproducibility or robustness. The absence of learning curves, hyperparameter sensitivity analysis, or failure cases raises concerns about the method's practicality. A model that achieves good results only under narrow conditions is less impactful than one that is broadly usable.

- Ambiguous role of the latent variable in multimodal generation: The paper claims that the latent variable z enables multimodal denoising, but the experimental setup does not isolate its effect beyond the ablation in Table 2. Specifically, it's unclear whether the diversity arises from z or from the stochasticity of the diffusion posterior q(x_{t-1}|x_t,x_0). The model in Eq. 6 combines both sources: z controls the generator output x_0, and then x_{t-1} is sampled from a Gaussian posterior. Without disentangling these contributions—e.g., by fixing z and varying the posterior noise, or vice versa—the claim that the method learns a 'multimodal denoising distribution' remains ambiguous. A controlled study would strengthen the mechanistic interpretation.

- No comparison to fast samplers at matched step counts: The paper claims a 2000× speedup over Song et al. (2021c), but this comparison uses a slow baseline (predictor-corrector with 2000 steps). To fairly assess progress, the method should be compared to state-of-the-art fast sampling techniques like DPM-Solver or DDIM at low step counts (e.g., 4–10 steps), where they also achieve strong FID scores. The current Table 1 includes DDIM at T=50 but not at T=4, making it impossible to judge whether the quality gain is due to the proposed architecture or simply more steps. The authors should report FID and recall for DDIM, DPM-Solver, or distilled models at T=4 on CIFAR-10 and plot these in Figure 4 to show where the proposed method stands on the speed-quality frontier.

- Lack of semantic diversity analysis on real image datasets: While the paper uses recall and StackedMNIST to argue for better mode coverage, it does not evaluate whether diversity extends to semantically meaningful variations in natural images. For example, on CelebA-HQ, one could measure the coverage of attributes (e.g., gender, glasses, hairstyle) and compare against StyleGAN2. A model might achieve high recall through texture-level variations without capturing high-level modes. The authors should perform a controlled experiment on CelebA or another attribute-labeled dataset, reporting the proportion of generated samples that span diverse attribute combinations, and compare against strong GAN baselines to substantiate the claim of superior mode coverage in realistic settings.

- Missing ablation on source of multimodality in denoising: The model in Eq. 6 combines two sources of randomness: the latent variable z and the stochastic posterior q(x_{t-1}|x_t,x_0). The ablation in Table 2 removes z but keeps the posterior sampling, making it unclear which component drives multimodality. To validate the core claim that z enables complex denoising distributions, the authors should add an experiment where z is fixed (e.g., z=0) but posterior noise is preserved, and another where z varies but the posterior is deterministic. Comparing sample diversity across these variants would isolate the contribution of the GAN-style latent code to multimodal generation.

- No guidance on training stability or hyperparameter sensitivity: Training a multi-step GAN-based diffusion model is inherently unstable, yet the paper provides no practical guidance on achieving convergence. Appendix D is cited for training stability but contains no details. Readers need to know: How sensitive is training to the number of discriminator updates per generator step? Are losses balanced across timesteps? Do later denoising steps require different learning rates? The authors should include learning curves for different steps, report failure modes (e.g., mode collapse in intermediate steps), and provide recommended hyperparameters for stable training to ensure reproducibility and practical adoption.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a theoretical argument or proof sketch showing that the adversarial training objective approximates the true reverse diffusion process or leads to consistent data distribution recovery.

- Add comparisons to state-of-the-art fast diffusion samplers (e.g., DDIM, DPM-Solver, progressive distillation) at low step counts (T ≤ 10) with both quantitative metrics and sampling time.

- Include a more rigorous analysis of mode coverage on high-resolution datasets (e.g., CelebA) using attribute-based clustering or human evaluation to validate diversity claims.

- Clarify and standardize the sampling time measurements, reporting per-image latency under consistent batch sizes and hardware conditions, and include full timing breakdowns.

- Expand the discussion of training stability with learning curves, hyperparameter sensitivity, and examples of failure modes or collapse behavior.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of DDIM's denoising distribution
comments.quote: In particular, Song et al. (2021a) uses  $\mathbf{x}_0$  sampling as a crucial ingredient to their method, but their denoising distribution is still a Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The claim that DDIM uses a Gaussian denoising distribution is incorrect. DDIM defines a non-Markovian reverse process that does not assume Gaussian transitions and can operate deterministically, decoupling the reverse process from Gaussian assumptions. By mischaracterizing this, the paper undermines its novelty argument for using non-Gaussian modeling. The statement should be corrected to acknowledge that DDIM already enables non-Gaussian or deterministic denoising paths, which weakens the claim that such modeling is uniquely enabled by the proposed method.

comments.title: Overlooks prior GAN-based diffusion modeling
comments.quote: Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) add an auxiliary adversarial loss to the main objective. This is fundamentally different from ours, as their auxiliary adversarial loss only acts as an image enhancer, and they do not use latent variables; therefore, the denoising distribution is still a unimodal Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The paper fails to cite Diffusion-GAN (Xiao et al., 2021), which directly models the reverse diffusion process using a conditional GAN with latent variables—precisely the core idea claimed as novel. While the auxiliary role of adversarial loss in Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. is correctly noted, the omission of a direct architectural predecessor overstates the method's originality. The related work section must acknowledge this prior work and clarify the distinctions, if any, in modeling or training strategy.

comments.title: Incorrect ELBO decomposition
comments.quote:
The ELBO can be written as matching the true denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ with the parameterized denoising model $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ using:

$\mathcal{L}=-\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\left[D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\|p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\right)\right]+C,$ (3)
comments.explanation: The ELBO does not involve minimizing KL divergence against the intractable $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$, as stated. The correct term uses the tractable $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)$, which has a closed-form Gaussian expression. This misstatement misrepresents the foundation of diffusion training and could mislead readers about the tractability of the objective. The text should be corrected to reflect the standard derivation, clarifying that $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is intractable and not used in practice.

comments.title: Ambiguous justification for multimodal denoising
comments.quote: The true denoising distribution for a small step size (i.e.,  $q(\mathbf{x}_4|\mathbf{x}_5 = X)$ ) is close to a Gaussian distribution. However, it becomes more complex and multimodal as the step size increases.
comments.explanation: The claim that $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ becomes multimodal for large steps lacks formal justification. If the data distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is unimodal, the reverse distribution remains unimodal regardless of step size. Multimodality arises only when $q(\mathbf{x}_0)$ itself is multimodal. The paper should clarify this dependency and provide a simple example (e.g., mixture of Gaussians) to ground the intuition, rather than presenting multimodality as a generic consequence of large steps.

comments.title: Divergence mismatch affects distributional consistency
comments.quote: In this case, $D_{\mathrm{adv}}$ takes a special instance of f-divergence called softened reverse KL *(Shannon et al., 2020)*, which is different from the forward KL divergence used in the original diffusion model training in Eq. 3.
comments.explanation: The use of softened reverse KL (mode-seeking) instead of forward KL (mode-covering) introduces a fundamental mismatch in training dynamics. This risks poor mode coverage in the reverse process, contradicting the paper's claims. While adversarial training can yield high-quality samples, the paper provides no analysis of whether the Markov chain induced by the GAN generator converges to the true data distribution. A discussion of this divergence-induced bias and its implications for global consistency is necessary.

comments.title: Joint sampling does not enable posterior sampling
comments.quote:
The first expectation requires sampling from $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ which is unknown. However, we use the identity $q(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{t-1})=\int d\mathbf{x}_{0}q(\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{0})=\int d\mathbf{x}_{0}q(\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ to rewrite the first expectation in Eq. 5 as:

$\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]=\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))].$
comments.explanation: The identity allows sampling from the joint $q(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_{t-1})$, but not from the conditional $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$. For discriminator training, one needs to condition on $\mathbf{x}_t$ and sample $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$, which forward sampling alone does not achieve. Without importance weighting, rejection sampling, or a learned encoder, the training data will not reflect the true conditional distribution. The paper must describe how conditional sampling is implemented, as this is critical for valid adversarial training.

comments.title: Overstated role of latent variable in multimodality
comments.quote: Removing latent variables  $\mathbf{z}$  converts our denoising model to a unimodal distribution. In the last line of Table 2, we study our model's performance without any latent variables  $\mathbf{z}$ . We see that the sample quality is significantly worse, suggesting the importance of multimodal denoising distributions.
comments.explanation: The claim that removing $\mathbf{z}$ makes the model unimodal is incorrect: the posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)$ remains stochastic and can produce multiple outputs. The ablation conflates two sources of randomness—latent $\mathbf{z}$ and posterior noise—leading to an unjustified attribution of multimodality. To support the claim, the authors should isolate the effects by fixing $\mathbf{z}$ while preserving posterior sampling, or vice versa, and compare diversity across variants.

comments.title: Contradictory FID interpretation in ablation
comments.quote: We observe that  $T = 4$  gives the best results, whereas there is a slight degrade in performance for larger  $T$ . We hypothesize that we may require a significantly higher capacity to accommodate larger  $T$ , as we need a conditional GAN for each denoising step.
comments.explanation: The claim that $T=4$ gives the 'best results' contradicts Table 2: $T=2$ achieves a lower FID (4.08) than $T=4$ (3.75), and FID is the primary quality metric. While $T=4$ has slightly higher recall, the text should acknowledge that $T=2$ outperforms on FID and clarify why $T=4$ is preferred overall. The current phrasing misrepresents the ablation results.

comments.title: Inconsistent sampling time comparison
comments.quote: our sampling time is about  $2000 \times$  faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and  $\sim 20 \times$  faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021).
comments.explanation: The 2000× speedup compares 0.21s (100 images) for the proposed model to 423.2s for Score SDE (VE), but the latter's time per image (4.23s) is implausibly slow for CIFAR-10. StyleGAN2 generates a single image in 0.04s, suggesting inconsistent batch sizes or unreported optimizations. The speed comparison lacks standardization—batch size, GPU utilization, and preprocessing must be clarified. Otherwise, the claimed speedup lacks credibility.

comments.title: Misleading FID reference set usage
comments.quote: We use the training set as a reference to compute the FID, following common practice in the literature (see Ho et al. (2020); Karras et al. (2019) as an example).
comments.explanation: Using the training set for FID computation violates standard protocol and risks rewarding overfitting. Ho et al. (2020) and Karras et al. (2019) use the test set, not the training set. Training-set FID can artificially lower scores, especially on small datasets like CIFAR-10. The reported FID of 3.75 may thus be biased. The authors must recompute FID using the test set and update results accordingly.

comments.title: FID comparison omits key GAN baselines
comments.quote:
Table 4: Generative results on CelebA-HQ-256

|  Model | FID↓  |
| --- | --- |
|  Denoising Diffusion GAN (ours) | 7.64  |
|  Score SDE (Song et al., 2021c) | 7.23  |

|  UDM (Kim et al., 2021) | 7.16  |

|  VAEBM (Xiao et al., 2021) | 20.4  |
|  NCP-VAE (Aneja et al., 2021) | 24.8  |

comments.explanation: The FID comparison omits StyleGAN2 and CIPS, which achieve FID ≈ 2.8 and 2.92 on CelebA-HQ, far below the proposed model's 7.64. By excluding these top-performing GANs, the paper creates a misleading impression of competitive quality. The claim of competitive performance among 'best GANs' is unsupported. These baselines must be included for a fair assessment.

comments.title: Overstated societal impact without evidence
comments.quote: Thus, we foresee that in the long term, our model can help with reducing the negative social impacts of existing generative models that fall short in capturing the data diversity.
comments.explanation: The claim that the model reduces negative social impacts is speculative and unsupported. The experiments use synthetic or standard benchmarks (CIFAR-10, StackedMNIST) without demographic or social attributes. High recall on synthetic modes does not imply fair representation of minorities. Without testing on socially relevant datasets or measuring bias, this statement overreaches. It should be removed or limited to technical mode coverage.

comments.title: Ambiguous reproducibility commitment
comments.quote: We currently provide experimental details in the appendices with a detailed list of hyperparameters and training settings. To aid with reproducibility further, we will release our source code publicly in the future with instructions to reproduce our results.
comments.explanation: Promising code release 'in the future' without a timeline or access mechanism undermines reproducibility. For a method involving multi-step GAN training and a 2000× speedup claim, immediate code availability is essential. The statement should be revised to confirm release at publication or provide a DOI to an archived repository.

comments.title: Ambiguous product indexing in forward process
comments.quote: q(\mathbf{x}_{1:T}|\mathbf{x}_{0})=\prod_{t\geq 1}q(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{t-1}),\quad q(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{t};\sqrt{1-\beta_{t}}\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\beta_{t}\mathbf{I}),$ (1)
comments.explanation: The product $\prod_{t\geq 1}$ suggests an infinite sequence, but the model uses finite $T$ steps. This contradicts the rest of the paper, which defines $\mathbf{x}_T$ and uses $t=1$ to $T$. The notation should be $\prod_{t=1}^T$ to match standard diffusion model conventions and avoid confusion.

comments.title: Time-dependent discriminator lacks architectural details
comments.quote: To set up the adversarial training, we denote the time-dependent discriminator as $D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t):\mathbb{R}^{N}\times\mathbb{R}^{N}\times\mathbb{R}\rightarrow[0,1]$, with parameters $\phi$. It takes the $N$-dimensional $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ and $\mathbf{x}_{t}$ as inputs, and decides whether $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ is a plausible denoised version of $\mathbf{x}_{t}$.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify how $t$ is incorporated into the discriminator—whether as a learned embedding, scalar input, or layer-wise conditioning. This is critical, as the denoising distribution changes with $t$. Without details on architecture or weight sharing across steps, the implementation cannot be reproduced. A description of the conditioning mechanism and ablation on design choices is needed.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes denoising diffusion GANs to address the generative learning trilemma by replacing Gaussian denoising distributions with multimodal GANs, claiming 2000x speedup while maintaining quality and diversity. While the approach is innovative and results impressive, there are significant concerns about the causal claims, scalability, and completeness of evaluation.

The paper presents an innovative hybrid approach combining diffusion models with GANs to enable few-step sampling. The 2000x speedup claim on CIFAR-10 is striking, and the ablation studies provide valuable insights into the method's components. However, the paper makes strong causal claims about the Gaussian assumption being the fundamental bottleneck without sufficient evidence, and the evaluation lacks critical scalability tests and fair comparisons to recent accelerated diffusion methods.

Overview issues:

- Unsubstantiated causal claim about Gaussian assumption: The paper claims that slow sampling in diffusion models is 'fundamentally attributed to the Gaussian assumption' in denoising (Sec 3.1). This strong causal claim lacks direct evidence. The authors show that the true denoising distribution becomes multimodal for large steps (Fig 2), but they don't demonstrate that this is the primary bottleneck versus other factors like the inherent difficulty of modeling complex distributions. To support this claim, they should include experiments comparing their multimodal approach against alternative methods that also relax the Gaussian assumption (e.g., mixture of Gaussians, normalizing flows) to isolate whether the multimodality or the adversarial training is the key factor. Without such comparisons, the claim remains speculative.

- Missing scalability validation beyond CIFAR-10: The 2000x speedup claim is only demonstrated on CIFAR-10, while higher-resolution results (Tables 4-5) show much smaller speed advantages. For CelebA-HQ and LSUN Church, the paper only reports FID scores without timing comparisons. This is a critical omission since computational challenges scale non-linearly with resolution. The authors must report sampling times for these higher-resolution datasets to verify whether the claimed speedups generalize. If the speedup diminishes significantly at 256x256 resolution, this would undermine the paper's claim of enabling 'real-world applications' which often require high-resolution outputs.

- Incomplete baseline comparisons for accelerated diffusion: The paper compares against several diffusion baselines but omits recent state-of-the-art accelerated methods like DDIM (Song et al., 2021a) and latent diffusion models. While DDIM appears in Table 1 with T=50, the paper doesn't explore DDIM's performance with very few steps (T=4-8) which would be the most relevant comparison. Similarly, latent diffusion approaches that operate in compressed spaces could achieve similar speedups. The authors need to include these critical baselines with comparable step counts to properly position their contribution. Without these comparisons, it's unclear whether the gains come from the multimodal modeling or simply from operating in a different space.

- Insufficient analysis of training stability: The paper mentions training stability is deferred to Appendix D, but GANs are notoriously sensitive to hyperparameters and can suffer from mode collapse. Given that the method requires training multiple conditional GANs (one per timestep), the stability challenges could be severe. The main text should include key findings about training stability, failure modes, and hyperparameter sensitivity. Specifically, readers need to know: (1) What is the success rate of training runs? (2) How sensitive is performance to the GAN architecture and loss functions? (3) Does the adversarial training become more unstable with larger T? These details are essential for assessing the method's practical applicability.

- Limited analysis of computational overhead: The paper claims 2000x speedup but doesn't adequately account for the computational overhead of the GAN components. Each denoising step now requires both a generator and discriminator network evaluation, plus sampling from the latent space. The authors should provide a detailed breakdown of where the speedup comes from - is it purely from fewer steps, or does the GAN architecture also contribute? A comparison of FLOPs per step between their method and standard diffusion models would help clarify the true computational efficiency. Without this analysis, the speedup claim may be misleading if it ignores the increased per-step complexity.

- Missing toy example demonstration: The paper would benefit from a concrete demonstration of its core insight using a simple, analytically tractable model. The authors claim that Gaussian denoising fails for large steps while multimodal distributions succeed, but they only show visualizations in Fig 2. A complete paper should include a worked example, perhaps using a 2D mixture of Gaussians dataset, where they can compute the true denoising distribution analytically for different step sizes and show quantitatively how the Gaussian approximation error grows with step size. This would provide concrete evidence for their theoretical motivation and help readers calibrate when multimodal denoising becomes necessary.

- Unverified recovery of diffusion baseline: The paper claims to generalize standard diffusion models but never demonstrates that their method recovers the original diffusion model as a special case. A crucial validation would be to show that when the conditional GAN generator is constrained to output deterministic predictions (no latent variable z), their method reduces to standard DDPM. The authors should run this experiment and verify that the FID scores match DDPM when using the same number of steps. Without this, readers cannot be certain that their framework truly encompasses standard diffusion models or whether it only works in the multimodal regime.

- Missing inference implementation details: The paper claims to enable real-world applications but provides no practical guidance on implementing their method for new tasks. Specifically, they don't discuss how to choose the number of denoising steps T for a given application, how the computational budget scales with T, or what architectural modifications are needed for different data types. A complete paper should include practical recommendations: e.g., start with T=4 for image synthesis, increase to T=8 for higher resolution, use the provided network architecture for images but modify for other modalities. Without this implementation roadmap, practitioners cannot easily adopt the method despite its claimed practical importance.

- Underdeveloped Pareto frontier analysis: The paper presents the FID vs time trade-off in Figure 4 but doesn't fully characterize the Pareto frontier of their method. A complete analysis should show how performance varies across different T values and different computational budgets, not just the optimal T=4 setting. The authors should plot FID and recall scores as a function of both T and sampling time to demonstrate where their method sits on the Pareto frontier compared to all baselines. This would help readers understand the full trade-off space and verify whether their method truly dominates existing approaches across different computational constraints.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a promising approach with impressive results on CIFAR-10, but makes strong claims that are not sufficiently supported by the experiments. The causal claims about the Gaussian assumption being the fundamental bottleneck lack evidence, scalability beyond 32x32 images is not demonstrated, and critical baseline comparisons are missing. These issues must be addressed before the contribution can be properly evaluated.

Revision targets:

- Add experiments comparing multimodal GAN denoising against alternative non-Gaussian approaches (e.g., mixture models, normalizing flows) to substantiate the causal claim about the Gaussian assumption

- Report sampling times and speedup factors for the 256x256 resolution experiments (CelebA-HQ, LSUN Church) to validate scalability claims

- Include comparisons with DDIM and latent diffusion models using comparable numbers of steps (T=4-8) as the most relevant baselines

- Move key training stability analysis from Appendix to main text, including success rates and hyperparameter sensitivity

- Provide detailed computational analysis breaking down FLOPs per step and accounting for GAN overhead in the speedup calculations

comments.title: Inconsistent speedup comparison methodology
comments.quote: In image generation, we observe that our model obtains sample quality and mode coverage competitive with diffusion models, while taking only as few as two denoising steps, achieving about 2000$\times$ speed-up in sampling compared to the predictor-corrector sampling by *Song et al. (2021c)* on CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The 2000× speedup claim compares your 2-step method against predictor-corrector sampling from Song et al. (2021c), but this creates an inconsistent comparison. Predictor-corrector sampling involves both a predictor (reverse SDE) and corrector steps, typically requiring many more evaluations than standard DDPM sampling. A fair comparison should be against the base diffusion model sampling method (DDPM) with the same number of function evaluations, or against accelerated methods like DDIM. The current comparison may exaggerate the speedup since predictor-corrector is inherently slower than standard diffusion sampling. Rewrite this as "achieving about 2000× speed-up compared to DDPM sampling with 1000 steps on CIFAR-10" to ensure consistency with standard diffusion baselines.

comments.title: Missing baseline comparison for DDIM with few steps
comments.quote: To better benchmark our method against existing diffusion models, we plot the FID score versus sampling time of diffusion models by varying the number of denoising steps (or the error tolerance for continuous-time models) in Figure 4.
comments.explanation: The paper only compares against DDIM with T=50 steps in Table 1, but this is not a meaningful comparison since your method uses T=4. For a fair benchmark of few-step diffusion, you should include DDIM results with T=4-8 steps. The current comparison creates a misleading impression that your method has an advantage simply because you're comparing against DDIM with many more steps. The Figure 4 plot should include DDIM curves down to T=4 steps to show whether your multimodal approach actually outperforms DDIM when both use comparable step counts.

comments.title: Missing sampling time data for high-resolution experiments
comments.quote: We report FID on these two datasets in Table 4 and 5. Similar to CIFAR-10, our model obtains competitive sample quality among the best diffusion models and GANs.
comments.explanation: You report FID scores for CelebA-HQ and LSUN Church at 256×256 resolution but provide no sampling time data. Your paper's central contribution is claimed speed improvements (2000× on CIFAR-10), yet you don't report whether similar speedups are achieved at higher resolutions. This is a critical omission since computational challenges scale non-linearly with resolution. Add sampling time measurements for these high-resolution experiments to demonstrate whether your claimed speed advantage generalizes beyond CIFAR-10.

comments.title: Inconsistent T=1 interpretation
comments.quote: Note that  $T = 1$  corresponds to training an unconditional GAN, as the conditioning  $\mathbf{x}_t$  contains almost no information about  $\mathbf{x}_0$ .
comments.explanation: The claim that T=1 corresponds to an unconditional GAN contradicts the paper's own framework. In Section 3.2, the authors state that T is assumed to be small (T≤8) and each diffusion step has larger β_t. For T=1, there is still a forward diffusion process from x_0 to x_1, meaning x_1 contains information about x_0 through the forward process q(x_1|x_0). The conditioning x_1 is not meaningless - it's a noisy version of x_0. This should be corrected to clarify that T=1 corresponds to a single-step conditional GAN, not an unconditional GAN. Rewrite as "Note that T=1 corresponds to a single-step conditional GAN, where the conditioning x_1 is a heavily noised version of x_0."

comments.title: Undefined divergence measure in training objective
comments.quote:
Our training is formulated by matching the conditional GAN generator $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ and $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ using an adversarial loss that minimizes a divergence $D_{\mathrm{adv}}$ per denoising step:

$\min_{\theta}\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\left[D_{\mathrm{adv}}(q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\|p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t}))\right],$ (4)
comments.explanation: Equation 4 introduces $D_{\mathrm{adv}}$ as a divergence measure but doesn't define it mathematically. While the text mentions it can be Wasserstein distance, Jensen-Shannon divergence, or f-divergence, the specific form used in the non-saturating GAN setup is not shown. The non-saturating GAN objective doesn't directly minimize a standard divergence but rather approximates one. Add after Eq. 4: "For non-saturating GANs, $D_{\mathrm{adv}}$ corresponds to the softened reverse KL divergence $D_{\mathrm{adv}}(p\|q) = \mathbb{E}_{p}[\log \frac{p}{q}] - \mathbb{E}_{p}[\log D_{\phi}]$ where $D_{\phi}$ is the optimal discriminator (Shannon et al., 2020)." because this clarifies the exact mathematical form of the divergence being minimized.

comments.title: Missing justification for ELBO equivalence
comments.quote: Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015) show that  $\mathcal{L}$  can be written in an alternative form with tractable distributions (see Appendix A of Ho et al. (2020) for details). Ho et al. (2020) show the equivalence of this form with score-based models trained with denoising score matching (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020).
comments.explanation:
The paper claims equivalence between different formulations but doesn't show the key mathematical steps. The transition from equation (3) to (4) requires applying Bayes' rule and using the Markov property, but this derivation is omitted. Readers need to see how $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ relates to $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0})$ through the intractable $q(\mathbf{x}_{0}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ term.

Add a brief derivation showing $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t}) = \int q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{0}|\mathbf{x}_{t})d\mathbf{x}_{0}$ and explain how conditioning on $\mathbf{x}_{0}$ in equation (4) avoids this intractable marginalization.

comments.title: Inconsistent performance characterization on LSUN Church
comments.quote: Similar to CIFAR-10, our model obtains competitive sample quality among the best diffusion models and GANs. In particular, in LSUN Church, our model outperforms DDPM and ImageBART (see Figure 7 and Appendix E for samples). Although, some GANs perform better on this dataset, their mode coverage is not reflected by the FID score.
comments.explanation: The characterization of performance on LSUN Church is inconsistent with the actual results in Table 5. Your model achieves FID 5.25, which is indeed better than DDPM (7.89) and ImageBART (7.32), but several GAN models perform significantly better: StyleGAN2 (3.86), CIPS (2.92), and StyleGAN (4.21). The statement that your model obtains "competitive sample quality among the best diffusion models and GANs" is misleading when your FID is 36% worse than the best GAN (StyleGAN2) and you rank 5th out of 8 models overall. Rewrite this as: "Similar to CIFAR-10, our model obtains competitive sample quality among diffusion models, outperforming DDPM and ImageBART on LSUN Church. While some GANs achieve better FID scores, our model may provide better mode coverage as FID does not fully capture this aspect."

comments.title: Unsubstantiated multimodality claim
comments.quote: This is the key difference that allows our denoising distribution $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ to become multimodal and complex in contrast to the unimodal denoising model in DDPM.
comments.explanation: The multimodality claim lacks mathematical justification. The marginal distribution $\int p(\mathbf{z})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0}=G_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{z},t))d\mathbf{z}$ is a mixture of Gaussians, but multimodality depends on specific properties of $G_{\theta}$. Add a brief explanation after this sentence: "Specifically, when the generator mapping $\mathbf{z} \mapsto \mathbf{x}_{0}$ is non-injective or creates multiple distinct regions in the latent space that map to different modes, the resulting mixture of Gaussians can exhibit multimodality. This occurs, for example, when $G_{\theta}$ is highly non-linear and different latent vectors $\mathbf{z}_1, \mathbf{z}_2$ produce $\mathbf{x}_0$ values that are sufficiently separated relative to the Gaussian posterior variance." because this provides the necessary mathematical conditions for multimodality.

comments.title: Incorrect marginal distribution description
comments.quote: The true denoising distribution  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$  can be written as  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t) \propto q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  using Bayes' rule where  $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  is the forward Gaussian diffusion shown in Eq. 1 and  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  is the marginal data distribution at step  $t$ .
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly describes $q(\mathbf{x}_{t - 1})$ as "the marginal data distribution at step $t$" when the notation clearly indicates it is the distribution at step $t-1$. This creates a contradiction between the mathematical notation and its verbal description. Rewrite the quoted text as "The true denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t - 1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ can be written as $q(\mathbf{x}_{t - 1}|\mathbf{x}_t) \propto q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t - 1})q(\mathbf{x}_{t - 1})$ using Bayes' rule where $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t - 1})$ is the forward Gaussian diffusion shown in Eq. 1 and $q(\mathbf{x}_{t - 1})$ is the marginal data distribution at step $t-1$" because the notation $q(\mathbf{x}_{t - 1})$ unambiguously refers to the distribution at timestep $t-1$.

comments.title: Missing definition of $\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}$
comments.quote: where $\tilde{\bm{\mu}}_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0})=\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_{t}}(1-\beta_{t})}{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t}}\mathbf{x}_{t}+\frac{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\beta_{t}}{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t}}\mathbf{x}_{0}$ and $\tilde{\beta}_{t}=\frac{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t}}\beta_{t}$. Here, $\alpha_{t}=1-\beta_{t}$ and $\bar{\alpha}_{t}=\prod_{s=1}^{t}\alpha_{s}$.
comments.explanation:
The first-pass reviewer correctly identifies that $\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}$ is used but not explicitly defined. While readers can infer the definition, explicit notation improves clarity, especially for $t=1$ where $\bar{\alpha}_{0} = 1$ by convention.

Add "and $\bar{\alpha}_{t-1} = \prod_{s=1}^{t-1}\alpha_{s}$ (with $\bar{\alpha}_{0} = 1$)" after the definition of $\bar{\alpha}_{t}$ to provide complete notation.

comments.title: Intractability reason for equation (3)
comments.quote: The objective above is intractable due to the unavailability of $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$.
comments.explanation:
The intractability stems from needing to marginalize over $\mathbf{x}_0$: $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t}) = \int q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{0}|\mathbf{x}_{t})d\mathbf{x}_{0}$, where $q(\mathbf{x}_{0}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ is intractable. Simply stating "unavailability" is imprecise.

Rewrite the sentence as "The objective above is intractable because computing $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ requires marginalizing over the unknown posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{0}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$" because this accurately identifies the source of intractability.

comments.title: Bracket mismatch in discriminator objective
comments.quote: $\min_{\phi}\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\big{[}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t)]+\mathbb{E}_{p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(1-D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]\big{]},$ (5)
comments.explanation: The term $\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t)]$ is missing its closing bracket. This creates ambiguity about the scope of the -log operator. Rewrite Eq. 5 as: $\min_{\phi}\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\big{[}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))] + \mathbb{E}_{p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(1-D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]\big{]}$ because the -log operator must clearly apply to the discriminator output within each expectation term.

comments.title: Incorrect expectation notation in identity derivation
comments.quote: $\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]=\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{0})q(\mathbf{x}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))].$
comments.explanation: The expectation notation $\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}$ is mathematically incorrect. While the expression $q(\mathbf{x}_{t})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ equals the joint distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$, the expectation notation should explicitly show the joint distribution to avoid confusion. Rewrite the expectation as $\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{t-1})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]$ because expectations are defined with respect to joint distributions, not products of conditional distributions.

comments.title: Ambiguous conditioning mechanism description
comments.quote: The conditioning  $\mathbf{x}_t$  is the input of the network, and time embedding is used to ensure conditioning on  $t$ . We let the latent variable  $\mathbf{z}$  control the normalization layers.
comments.explanation: The description of the conditioning mechanism is ambiguous. If $\mathbf{x}_t$ is already the network input, it's unclear why an additional time embedding is needed for conditioning on $t$. This could confuse readers about whether the timestep information is already embedded in $\mathbf{x}_t$ or needs to be added separately. The authors should clarify whether $\mathbf{x}_t$ inherently contains timestep information or if the time embedding provides additional conditioning beyond what's in $\mathbf{x}_t$.

comments.title: Inconsistent speedup calculation in Table 1
comments.quote: For example, our sampling time is about  $2000 \times$  faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and  $\sim 20 \times$  faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021).
comments.explanation: The 2000x speedup claim against Score SDE models is inconsistent with the data in Table 1. The table shows Score SDE (VE) takes 423.2 seconds while your model takes 0.21 seconds, which is 423.2/0.21 ≈ 2015x, not 2000x. More importantly, the 20x speedup against FastDDPM is incorrect - FastDDPM takes 4.01 seconds while your model takes 0.21 seconds, giving 4.01/0.21 ≈ 19x, not 20x. While these are small discrepancies, precision matters when making such strong quantitative claims. Rewrite the sentence as: "For example, our sampling time is about 2015× faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and 19× faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021)."

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Denoising Diffusion GANs as a solution to the generative learning trilemma, replacing the standard Gaussian denoising step with a conditional GAN to allow for larger step sizes and faster sampling. While the empirical results are impressive, the framework introduces significant concerns regarding training stability and the theoretical justification of the adversarial objective within the diffusion chain.

The paper makes a strong contribution by demonstrating that diffusion-based generative models can be accelerated significantly using adversarial training. The empirical results on CIFAR-10 are compelling and clearly show a favorable trade-off between sampling speed and sample quality. However, the paper lacks sufficient analysis of the stability of the proposed adversarial training objective across different noise levels.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Stability of Adversarial Training Across Noise Levels: The proposed method (Eq. 4 and 5) applies an adversarial loss at each denoising step. Since the distribution of the noise changes significantly as the process moves from $T$ to $0$, the discriminator $D_{\phi}$ must adapt to vastly different data manifolds. The paper does not provide evidence that the adversarial training remains stable or that the discriminator provides a consistent gradient signal across these disparate noise levels. Without an analysis of the discriminator's behavior or gradient flow as $t$ varies, it is difficult to determine if the training is robust or if it relies on specific, fragile hyperparameter tuning for each step.

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for the Multimodal Approximation: The authors argue that the Gaussian assumption in standard diffusion models is the root cause of slow sampling and that a multimodal GAN generator solves this. However, the paper does not formally characterize the complexity of the true posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ or prove that a conditional GAN is the appropriate inductive bias to capture it. The claim that the distribution becomes 'more complex and multimodal' as step size increases is intuitive, but the paper lacks a rigorous argument for why this specific GAN-based parameterization is well-posed for the reverse diffusion process.

- Potential for Mode Collapse in Adversarial Denoising: One of the primary motivations for using diffusion models is their superior mode coverage compared to traditional GANs. By introducing an adversarial component into every step of the denoising chain, the paper risks re-introducing the mode collapse issues that diffusion models were designed to avoid. While the authors demonstrate good results on StackedMNIST and CIFAR-10, they do not address whether the adversarial objective might cause the model to ignore certain modes of the data distribution over the course of the iterative denoising process. A more thorough investigation into whether the model preserves the full support of the data distribution compared to standard DDPM would be necessary.

- Insufficient Evaluation of Sampling Sensitivity: The paper reports results for a fixed number of steps ($T=4$). However, the sensitivity of the model to the choice of $T$ during inference is not fully explored. If the model is trained with a specific $T$, it is unclear how it behaves when the number of steps is changed at test time, or if the model requires re-training for different step counts. A more detailed ablation on the relationship between $T$ and the FID/Recall metrics would clarify if the model is truly flexible or if it is overfitted to a specific, small number of steps.

- Lack of analysis on the impact of training step count: The paper claims that modeling the denoising distribution as a multimodal GAN enables large-step denoising. However, the methodology section suggests that the model is trained for a fixed T (e.g., T=4). It is unclear if the model can be used for different step counts at inference time without re-training, or how the learned denoising kernels perform when the discretization schedule is altered. A systematic evaluation comparing FID and Recall for varying T in {2, 4, 8, 16} using the same model weights would demonstrate whether the proposed approach truly provides a flexible sampling framework or if it is constrained to the specific discretization used during training.

- Absence of a formal comparison of mode coverage dynamics: The paper argues that its model recovers better mode coverage than traditional GANs while maintaining the speed of diffusion.
