While the authors use StackedMNIST to show mode coverage, they do not track how the distribution of generated samples changes across the iterative denoising steps. A visualization or quantitative metric (such as the evolution of the KL divergence or coverage metrics) at each step t=T, ..., 0 would clarify whether mode collapse occurs during the initial, high-noise denoising steps or if the GAN-based kernels successfully maintain diversity throughout the entire reverse process.

- Need for a controlled comparison against a non-adversarial large-step baseline: The paper attributes the success of Denoising Diffusion GANs to the multimodal GAN parameterization. To isolate the contribution of the GAN objective from the architectural changes (like the latent variable conditioning), the authors should compare their performance against a baseline that uses the same U-Net architecture and latent-variable conditioning but is trained with a standard regression loss (e.g., MSE on x_0 or epsilon). This would empirically verify that the adversarial loss is necessary to capture the multimodality of the posterior rather than the architectural capacity alone.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a more detailed analysis or empirical evidence regarding the stability of the discriminator gradient across different noise levels during the iterative training process.

- Include a more comprehensive ablation study on the sensitivity of the model to the number of denoising steps $T$ during inference.

- Add a discussion or experiment comparing the mode coverage of the proposed model against standard diffusion models using metrics beyond FID and simple recall, such as MS-SSIM or a more granular analysis of mode capture.

- Clarify the mathematical relationship between the softened reverse KL divergence used in the GAN objective and the standard ELBO objective of diffusion models.

comments.title: Inaccuracy in ELBO objective definition
comments.quote:
The ELBO can be written as matching the true denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ with the parameterized denoising model $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ using:

$\mathcal{L}=-\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\left[D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\|p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\right)\right]+C,$ (3)
comments.explanation: Equation (3) is mathematically imprecise because the true denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is generally intractable. The standard variational lower bound used in diffusion models is defined by the KL divergence between the posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)$ and the model $p_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$. By conditioning on $\mathbf{x}_0$, the posterior becomes tractable, which is the actual objective optimized in practice. Please rewrite Equation (3) to reflect the conditional posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0)$ to align with the standard derivation.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding speed-up factor
comments.quote: In image generation, we observe that our model obtains sample quality and mode coverage competitive with diffusion models, while taking only as few as two denoising steps, achieving about 2000$\times$ speed-up in sampling compared to the predictor-corrector sampling by *Song et al. (2021c)* on CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The paper claims a 2000x speed-up compared to the predictor-corrector sampler from Song et al. (2021c). However, the PC sampler is typically evaluated with 1000 steps, and a 2-step model would theoretically yield a 500x speed-up, not 2000x, assuming similar per-step compute costs. Please clarify if the 2000x figure is derived from wall-clock time comparisons or step counts, and reconcile this with the stated 2-step sampling process.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b)
comments.quote: Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) add an auxiliary adversarial loss to the main objective. This is fundamentally different from ours, as their auxiliary adversarial loss only acts as an image enhancer, and they do not use latent variables; therefore, the denoising distribution is still a unimodal Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The claim that Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) do not use latent variables is inaccurate. Their work, 'Adversarial Score-based Generative Modeling', explicitly utilizes a score-based model which operates on the latent space of the diffusion process, and their formulation involves conditioning on the noisy latent variable at each step. Please revise this description to accurately reflect their methodology.

comments.title: Inconsistency in performance degradation for larger T
comments.quote:
sity. When varying  $T > 1$ , we observe that  $T = 4$  gives the best results, whereas there is a slight degrade in performance for larger  $T$ . We hypothesize that we may require a significantly higher capacity to accommodate larger  $T$ , as we need a conditional GAN for each denoising step.

Diffusion as
comments.explanation: The hypothesis that larger $T$ requires higher capacity is inconsistent with the architecture of diffusion models, where the same network is typically reused across all time steps. If the model uses a separate GAN for each step, the capacity scales linearly with $T$, which contradicts the claim that a 'higher capacity' is needed to accommodate larger $T$. Please clarify if the model uses shared weights across steps or distinct weights for each step, as this significantly impacts the interpretation of the performance degradation.

comments.title: Correction of marginal distribution index in Bayes' rule
comments.quote: d  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  is the marginal data distribution at step  $
comments.explanation: By Bayes' theorem, the posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is proportional to the likelihood $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ multiplied by the prior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. The term $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ represents the marginal distribution of the data at time step $t-1$, not $t$. Please rewrite 'marginal data distribution at step $t$' as 'marginal data distribution at step $t-1$' to ensure consistency with standard notation.

comments.title: Misinterpretation of posterior parameter dependence
comments.quote: The distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{0},\mathbf{x}_{t})$ is intuitively the distribution over $\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$ when denoising from $\mathbf{x}_{t}$ towards $\mathbf{x}_{0}$, and it always has a Gaussian form for the diffusion process in Eq. 1, independent of the step size and complexity of the data distribution
comments.explanation: The parameters of the Gaussian $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{0}, \mathbf{x}_{t})$ are functions of $\beta_t$. Since $\beta_t$ defines the step size, the distribution is explicitly dependent on it. The authors likely meant that the functional form (Gaussian) is invariant to the choice of data distribution, but the phrasing 'independent of the step size' is mathematically incorrect. Please remove this phrase to avoid inaccuracy.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes denoising diffusion GANs to address the generative learning trilemma by replacing Gaussian denoising steps with multimodal conditional GANs. The approach aims to drastically reduce sampling steps while maintaining quality and diversity. However, the manuscript contains overstated speedup claims, lacks critical training stability details, and oversimplifies the theoretical motivation by ignoring modern ODE/SDE solvers. Reproducibility is also weakened by the absence of multi-seed statistics and code.

The paper tackles a relevant problem by hybridizing diffusion and GAN architectures to accelerate sampling. The empirical results on CIFAR-10 and higher-resolution datasets demonstrate competitive fidelity and improved recall scores compared to standard GANs. The core idea of using a latent variable to induce multimodality in the denoising step is intuitive and aligns with recent trends in fast diffusion sampling.

Overview issues:

- Sensationalized Speedup Claim and Inconsistent Baseline Comparison: The abstract claims a $2000\times$ speedup over Song et al. (2021c), yet Table 1 reveals that modern deterministic samplers like DDIM and FastDDPM require only $\sim 4$ seconds for 50 steps, making the actual speedup roughly $20\times$. Comparing against the slowest predictor-corrector implementation rather than standard ODE solvers misrepresents the practical advantage. Figure 4 plots FID versus sampling time, but the curve for diffusion models is sparse and does not account for recent solvers that achieve comparable fidelity in fewer steps. This discrepancy weakens the central selling point of the method and requires a recalibration of the speedup narrative against a more representative set of fast-sampling baselines.

- Missing Training Stability Analysis and Reproducibility Details: Adversarial training for stepwise denoising is notoriously unstable, yet the paper provides no concrete details on how the adversarial loss in Eq. 4 is balanced across steps or how the discriminator is initialized. Section 3.2 describes a single network with time embedding, but Appendix D (referenced for stability) is absent from the provided text. The reproducibility statement promises future code release rather than supplying exact hyperparameters or multiple random seeds. Without variance reporting across independent runs, it is impossible to verify whether the reported FID and recall scores are robust or the result of lucky initialization. The authors must provide a complete training recipe and statistical reporting to substantiate the empirical claims.

- Oversimplified Theoretical Premise Regarding Gaussian Assumptions: The core motivation asserts that slow sampling is fundamentally caused by the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step. This overlooks the fact that deterministic samplers (e.g., DDIM, DPM-Solvers) bypass the Gaussian step assumption by directly solving the underlying ODE/SDE, achieving high quality with far fewer steps. By framing the Gaussian assumption as the primary bottleneck, the paper dismisses a major class of fast-sampling methods that do not rely on multimodal distribution learning. The introduction should acknowledge these solvers and clarify why modeling a multimodal denoising distribution offers a distinct advantage over improved numerical integration.

- Superficial Ablation on Step Count and Capacity Trade-offs: Table 2 shows that $T=4$ yields the best performance, while $T=8$ degrades results. The authors attribute this to a need for higher capacity for larger $T$, but this hypothesis is not tested or supported by additional experiments. The paper does not investigate whether the degradation stems from optimization difficulty, discriminator overpowering at higher noise levels, or insufficient generator expressiveness. A more rigorous ablation isolating step size, network depth, and latent variable usage would clarify the practical limits of the approach and justify the choice of $T=4$.

- Unaddressed Conditioning and Discriminator Design for Stepwise GANs: The discriminator takes $(x_{t-1}, x_t, t)$ as input, while the generator conditions on $(x_t, z, t)$ as described in Sec. 3.2. The paper does not explain how the discriminator handles the varying noise levels across steps or whether it requires separate training runs for each $t$. Training a single discriminator to distinguish real denoised samples from generated ones across a wide range of noise schedules is a non-trivial challenge that typically requires careful normalization or adaptive architectures. Without discussing how the discriminator avoids overfitting to specific noise levels or how gradient flow is balanced across steps, the method's scalability remains unclear.

- Missing comparison to modern few-step diffusion baselines: The abstract and introduction claim that DDGAN is the first model to reduce sampling cost enough for real-world applications. This claim overlooks recent advances in consistency models and rectified flow, which also achieve high-quality generation in a few steps. The paper does not position DDGAN against these modern few-step samplers, leaving a gap in the literature review and empirical evaluation. Readers will wonder whether the proposed GAN-based denoising offers distinct advantages over deterministic consistency distillation or flow matching. To address this, the authors should add a comparison table or discussion section benchmarking DDGAN against consistency models and rectified flow on CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ. This would clarify whether the multimodal GAN approach provides superior mode coverage or stability compared to these newer deterministic methods.

- Missing per-step computational cost and FLOPs analysis: The paper attributes the 2000x speedup solely to the reduction in denoising steps, but it does not account for the computational density of each step. GAN generators typically involve more complex architectures and higher FLOPs per forward pass than standard diffusion networks. Without a breakdown of FLOPs or parameter counts per step, the wall-clock speedup claim is incomplete and hardware-dependent. The authors should report the FLOPs required for a single GAN denoising step and compare it to a diffusion step. This computation would show whether the total inference cost is genuinely lower or if the speedup comes primarily from fewer iterations. Providing this metric is essential to substantiate the claim of practical, low-cost deployment.

- Missing worked parametric example for multimodal posteriors: The theoretical motivation relies on the assertion that large denoising steps produce multimodal posterior distributions that a Gaussian approximation cannot capture. The paper illustrates this with a 1D visualization and a 2D Gaussian grid, but it does not work through a fully specified parametric model. A concrete example using a simple mixture distribution, such as a two-component Gaussian mixture, would let readers verify the theory analytically. The authors should compute the exact posterior q(x_{t-1}|x_t) for this mixture at a large step size and show explicitly how the Gaussian approximation collapses modes while the GAN formulation preserves them. Adding this worked example would ground the theoretical claims and clarify the conditions under which the proposed method is necessary.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Recalculate and restate the speedup claim using fair comparisons against modern ODE/SDE solvers (e.g., DDIM, DPM-Solver) and report wall-clock times under identical hardware conditions.

- Provide complete training details for the adversarial objective in Eq. 4, including loss weighting, discriminator architecture, normalization strategies, and a discussion of convergence behavior.

- Report evaluation metrics (FID, IS, Recall) with standard deviations across at least three independent random seeds to demonstrate statistical robustness.

- Expand the ablation studies in Table 2 to analyze why $T=8$ degrades performance, testing hypotheses related to network capacity, noise level conditioning, and optimization stability.

comments.title: Incorrect sufficient condition for Gaussian denoising distribution
comments.quote: Second, if data marginal  $q(\mathbf{x}_t)$  is Gaussian, the denoising distribution  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$  is also a Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: This claim is mathematically flawed. By Bayes' rule, $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t) \propto q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. The likelihood $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ is Gaussian, but the posterior's shape depends on the prior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. If the prior is non-Gaussian, the product remains non-Gaussian regardless of the forward marginal. The normalization constant $q(\mathbf{x}_t)$ does not determine the posterior's functional form. The correct sufficient condition is that the prior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ itself is Gaussian. Please correct this derivation to avoid misleading readers about when the Gaussian approximation holds.

comments.title: Contradiction between capacity hypothesis and model architecture
comments.quote:
$T$ . We hypothesize that we may require a significantly higher capacity to accommodate larger  $T$ , as we need a conditional GAN for each denoising step.

Diff
comments.explanation: Section 3.2 explicitly describes using a single generator network with a time embedding to handle all denoising steps, rather than training separate GANs per step. If capacity does not scale with $T$, this hypothesis rests on a false architectural premise. The performance drop at $T=8$ likely stems from optimization difficulty or discriminator overpowering at higher noise levels. Please revise this explanation to match the single-network implementation and clarify the actual bottleneck.

comments.title: Recall score claim contradicts Table 1 data
comments.quote: In contrast, our model obtains a significantly better recall score, even higher than several advanced likelihood-based models, and competitive among diffusion models.
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports a recall score of 0.57 for the proposed method. This value is lower than Score SDE (VP) at 0.59 and LSGM at 0.61. It only exceeds DDIM (0.53) and FastDDPM (0.56). The text overstates the performance relative to the baselines listed in the table. Please adjust the claim to accurately reflect the reported numbers.

comments.title: Incorrect reference dataset for FID computation
comments.quote: We use the training set as a reference to compute the FID, following common practice in the literature (see Ho et al. (2020); Karras et al. (2019) as an example).
comments.explanation: The cited works actually use the CIFAR-10 test set as the reference distribution for FID, not the training set. Using the training set as a reference is non-standard and can artificially inflate FID scores due to overfitting to the reference distribution. Please clarify the reference dataset used or correct the citation to match standard benchmarking protocols.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of DDIM's reverse process assumption
comments.quote: In particular, Song et al. (2021a) uses  $\mathbf{x}_0$  sampling as a crucial ingredient to their method, but their denoising distribution is still a Gaussian.
comments.explanation: This statement misrepresents Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM). DDIM explicitly abandons the Markov assumption and formulates the reverse process as a deterministic ordinary differential equation (ODE), rather than assuming a Gaussian transition distribution. By parameterizing the reverse process directly in terms of the predicted clean data, DDIM bypasses the Gaussian assumption entirely. Stating that its denoising distribution remains Gaussian overlooks this fundamental methodological shift. Please clarify that DDIM achieves speed via deterministic ODE integration.

comments.title: Flawed causal link between latent variables and distribution modality
comments.quote: Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) add an auxiliary adversarial loss to the main objective. This is fundamentally different from ours, as their auxiliary adversarial loss only acts as an image enhancer, and they do not use latent variables; therefore, the denoising distribution is still a unimodal Gaussian.
comments.explanation: The assertion that the absence of latent variables directly causes the denoising distribution to remain unimodal is logically unsound. The modality of the denoising distribution is determined by the forward diffusion process and the reverse process parameterization, not by whether the model operates in pixel or latent space. Many pixel-space diffusion models could theoretically approximate multimodal posteriors if explicitly designed to do so. Please decouple the discussion of latent spaces from the Gaussian assumption to avoid conflating architectural choices with distributional properties.

comments.title: Conclusion overstates trilemma resolution despite ablation results
comments.quote: Our denoising diffusion GAN overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent, allowing diffusion models to be applied to real-world problems with low computational cost.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the method overcomes the trilemma, yet the ablation studies show performance degradation when increasing step counts from $T=4$ to $T=8$. Adversarial training across noise levels also introduces stability concerns, and GAN inference carries higher per-step computational density than diffusion steps. Claiming to overcome the trilemma and enable real-world deployment with 'low computational cost' ignores these documented limitations. The conclusion should accurately reflect the method's current standing as a promising but constrained approach.

comments.title: Conflation of marginal and conditional posteriors in ELBO
comments.quote:
The ELBO can be written as matching the true denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ with the parameterized denoising model $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$ using:

$\mathcal{L}=-\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\left[D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\|p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})\right)\right]+C,$ (3)
comments.explanation: In the standard variational derivation for diffusion models, the KL divergence term actually involves the conditional posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t}, \mathbf{x}_0)$, not the marginal $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})$. The marginal posterior is indeed intractable, but the ELBO is constructed specifically to use the conditional posterior, which is analytically available via Bayes' rule. Writing Eq. (3) with the marginal distribution is mathematically imprecise and obscures the mechanism that makes the bound tractable. Please revise Eq. (3) and the surrounding text to explicitly use the conditional form.

comments.title: Misattribution of normalization type in cited GAN architectures
comments.quote: In particular, we replace all group normalization layers (Wu & He, 2018) in NCSN++ with adaptive group normalization layers in the generator, similar to Karras et al. (2019); Huang & Belongie (2017)
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their adaptive group normalization is 'similar to Karras et al. (2019); Huang & Belongie (2017)'. This claim misrepresents the cited architectures. Both StyleGAN and SPADE rely on adaptive instance normalization, not group normalization. Instance normalization computes statistics per channel, while group normalization partitions channels into subgroups. Citing these works as direct precedents for adaptive group normalization creates a technical inaccuracy. Please clarify that you are adapting the adaptive instance normalization paradigm to group normalization, or remove the direct comparison.

comments.title: Imprecise claim regarding reverse process functional form
comments.quote: the product in the Bayes' rule is dominated by  $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  and the reversal of the diffusion process takes an identical functional form as the forward process (Feller, 1949).
comments.explanation: The assertion that the reverse process takes an 'identical functional form' as the forward process is mathematically imprecise. While both are diffusion processes, the reverse SDE has a drift term $f(\mathbf{x}) - g(t)^2 \nabla \log q_t(\mathbf{x})$, whereas the forward SDE has drift $f(\mathbf{x})$. They only coincide if the score function vanishes, which is not generally true. The reverse process shares the same diffusion coefficient but features a modified drift derived from the data marginal's score. Please clarify that the reverse process is a diffusion with a different drift term rather than an identical functional form.

comments.title: Unjustified claim on step size and multimodality
comments.quote: We see that as the denoising step gets larger, the true denoising distribution becomes more complex and multimodal.
comments.explanation: The statement that the denoising distribution 'becomes more complex and multimodal' as the step size increases lacks a precise mathematical justification. In the limit of a very large step size ($\beta_t \to 1$), the likelihood $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ becomes nearly uniform, causing the posterior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ to converge directly to the prior $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. Thus, the posterior does not inherently become 'more complex'; rather, it simply recovers the structure of the prior distribution at that time step. Please clarify that the multimodality arises from the prior itself, and large steps merely reduce the smoothing effect of the Gaussian likelihood.

comments.title: Mismatched citation for the predictor-corrector baseline
comments.quote: For example, our sampling time is about  $2000 \times$  faster than the predictor-corrector sampling by Song et al. (2021c) and  $\sim 20 \times$  faster than FastDDPM (Kong & Ping, 2021).
comments.explanation: Table 1 and the surrounding text compare against Score SDE (VP) from Song et al. (2021c), not the predictor-corrector sampler. The predictor-corrector method was introduced in Song et al. (2021a) and (2021b). Please correct the citation to accurately reflect the baseline used in the experiments.

comments.title: Mislabeling T=1 as an unconditional GAN
comments.quote: Note that  $T = 1$  corresponds to training an unconditional GAN, as the conditioning  $\mathbf{x}_t$  contains almost no information about  $\mathbf{x}_0$ . We obser
comments.explanation: The architecture explicitly takes $\mathbf{x}_t$ as input, which classifies the model as a conditional GAN regardless of how much information $\mathbf{x}_t$ carries about $\mathbf{x}_0$. Calling it unconditional contradicts the model definition and confuses the reader about the training setup. Please revise the terminology to 'conditional GAN conditioned on a highly noisy image' to preserve technical accuracy.

comments.title: Conflation of statistical diversity and demographic fairness
comments.quote: Mode coverage and high diversity are key requirements for reducing biases in generative models and improving the representation of minorities in a population.
comments.explanation: This sentence incorrectly equates statistical diversity with demographic fairness. A model that achieves high mode coverage will faithfully reproduce whatever biases or underrepresentation exist in its training data. If minority groups are underrepresented in the dataset, increasing diversity will not improve their representation; it will only ensure the model covers the existing skewed distribution more thoroughly. Please clarify that high diversity preserves the distributional coverage of the training data rather than inherently correcting demographic biases.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a hybrid diffusion-GAN model that achieves impressive speedups on CIFAR-10 while maintaining competitive sample quality and diversity. The core idea—replacing Gaussian denoising with a multimodal conditional GAN to enable larger step sizes—is conceptually sound and well-motivated. The empirical results are strong, and the ablation studies provide useful insights. However, several methodological concerns and framing issues need to be addressed before the paper can be considered for acceptance.

Overview issues:

- Unsubstantiated claim that Gaussian assumption is the sole cause of slow sampling: The paper argues that slow sampling in diffusion models is 'fundamentally attributed to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step' (Abstract, Section 1). This is an overstatement. While the Gaussian assumption indeed limits step size in standard formulations, other factors such as discretization error, model capacity, and the noise schedule also contribute to the need for many steps. The paper does not provide controlled experiments that isolate the effect of the Gaussian assumption from these other factors. For instance, one could keep the Gaussian assumption but use a more expressive mean function or a learned noise schedule to potentially reduce steps. The claim would be stronger if the authors demonstrated that, holding everything else fixed, replacing the Gaussian with a multimodal distribution is the key enabler of large-step denoising. A suggested fix is to temper the language: instead of 'fundamentally attributed,' say 'a key contributing factor is' or 'we hypothesize that.'

- Insufficient validation that the conditional GAN captures the true multimodal denoising distribution: The paper assumes that a conditional GAN can accurately model the true denoising distribution q(x_{t-1}|x_t) for large steps. However, GANs are known to suffer from mode collapse and training instability, which could cause the learned distribution to be a mode-seeking approximation rather than a faithful representation of the true multimodal distribution. The paper provides some evidence (Figure 8, ablation on latent variables) but does not directly measure how well p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t) matches q(x_{t-1}|x_t). For example, one could compute the Wasserstein distance or KL divergence between samples from the learned and true denoising distributions on a simple dataset like 25-Gaussians. Without such validation, it is unclear whether the GAN is truly capturing multimodality or merely producing diverse samples that happen to look plausible. The authors should add a quantitative comparison of the learned vs. true denoising distributions, at least for the toy dataset.

- Lack of statistical significance and confidence intervals for main results: The quantitative results in Tables 1, 2, 4, and 5 report single numbers for each metric without any indication of variability across runs. GAN training is notoriously sensitive to random seeds and hyperparameters, and the reported FID/IS/Recall values could vary significantly across different training runs. The paper does not state how many runs were performed, nor does it provide standard deviations or confidence intervals. This is a critical omission for an empirical paper making bold claims (e.g., 2000× speedup). The authors should report metrics averaged over at least 3-5 independent runs with standard deviations, and ideally perform a statistical test to confirm that the proposed method significantly outperforms baselines on key metrics.

- Generalization to higher-resolution datasets is not demonstrated: The 2000× speedup claim is based on CIFAR-10 (32×32 images). The paper also reports results on CelebA-HQ and LSUN Church at 256×256, but the speedup factor is not reported for these datasets. Moreover, the FID scores on these datasets are competitive but not state-of-the-art (e.g., StyleGAN2 achieves FID 3.86 on LSUN Church vs. 5.25 for the proposed method). It is unclear whether the speedup advantage holds at higher resolutions, where the denoising distribution may be even more complex and the GAN may struggle with stability. The paper should report sampling time and NFE for the high-resolution experiments, and discuss any degradation in speedup or quality. Without this, the claim of enabling 'real-world applications inexpensively' is premature.

- Incomplete comparison with recent acceleration methods: The paper compares against several diffusion model acceleration techniques (DDIM, FastDDPM, distillation) but misses important recent methods such as Consistency Models (Song et al., 2023) and DPM-Solver (Lu et al., 2022). While the paper was published in 2022, the literature context provided indicates that Consistency Models achieve one-step generation with FID 3.55 on CIFAR-10, which is competitive with the proposed method's FID 3.75 using 4 steps. The paper should at least acknowledge these methods and discuss how the proposed approach compares in terms of speed-quality trade-off. If the paper is being reviewed for a current venue, a comparison with these methods is essential to demonstrate the contribution's relevance.

- The generative learning trilemma framing is oversimplified: The paper presents the trilemma as a universal challenge where models must trade off sample quality, mode coverage, and sampling speed. While this is a useful heuristic, it oversimplifies the landscape. For instance, some models (e.g., VAEs) can achieve fast sampling and good mode coverage but poor quality, while others (e.g., GANs) achieve fast sampling and good quality but poor coverage. The trilemma implies that no model can excel at all three, but the paper's own results show that the proposed model achieves competitive quality and coverage with fast sampling, effectively 'solving' the trilemma. This raises the question: is the trilemma a fundamental trade-off or just a description of existing models' limitations? The paper should clarify that the trilemma is an empirical observation about current methods, not a theoretical impossibility, and discuss whether the proposed model truly overcomes it or merely shifts the trade-off (e.g., to training time or memory).

- No controlled experiment isolating the Gaussian assumption: The paper claims that the Gaussian assumption is the fundamental cause of slow sampling, but it never runs a controlled experiment that keeps everything else fixed and only replaces the Gaussian denoising distribution with a multimodal one. Without such an experiment, the claim remains an untested hypothesis. The authors should design an ablation where, for the same step size and model capacity, they compare a Gaussian denoising model (e.g., a DDPM with a learned covariance) against their GAN-based multimodal model, measuring the resulting FID and sampling steps. This would directly test whether multimodality, rather than other factors like the adversarial objective or network architecture, is the key enabler of large-step denoising.

- Missing quantitative validation of learned denoising distribution: The paper assumes that the conditional GAN faithfully approximates the true denoising distribution q(x_{t-1}|x_t) for large steps, but it provides no quantitative measure of how well the learned distribution matches the true one. On a simple dataset like 25-Gaussians, the true denoising distribution can be computed analytically or via Monte Carlo. The authors should compute a distributional distance (e.g., Wasserstein-2 or maximum mean discrepancy) between samples from p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t) and q(x_{t-1}|x_t) for several values of t. Without this validation, it is unclear whether the GAN is capturing the true multimodality or merely generating diverse but incorrect samples.

- No discussion of estimation or inference for the denoising distribution: The paper proposes modeling the denoising distribution with a conditional GAN but provides no guidance on how to estimate it reliably in practice. GAN training is notoriously unstable and sensitive to hyperparameters, yet the paper does not discuss convergence diagnostics, mode collapse detection, or practical tips for stabilizing the adversarial training across multiple denoising steps. For a paper that claims practical relevance, this is a notable gap. The authors should add a section or appendix describing training stability measures (e.g., gradient penalties, spectral normalization, learning rate schedules) and reporting the number of training runs that succeeded versus failed.

- Speedup claim not verified on high-resolution datasets: The 2000× speedup is reported only on CIFAR-10 (32×32). On CelebA-HQ and LSUN Church (256×256), the paper reports FID but omits sampling time and number of function evaluations. Without these numbers, it is impossible to assess whether the speedup generalizes to higher resolutions, where the denoising distribution may be more complex and the GAN may require more steps or larger networks. The authors should report NFE and wall-clock time for the high-resolution experiments, and discuss any degradation in speedup relative to CIFAR-10. This is essential to support the claim that the model enables 'real-world applications inexpensively.'

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add statistical significance measures (standard deviations, multiple runs) for all quantitative results, especially FID, IS, and Recall on CIFAR-10.

- Provide a direct quantitative comparison between the learned denoising distribution p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t) and the true distribution q(x_{t-1}|x_t) on a simple dataset (e.g., 25-Gaussians) to validate that the GAN captures multimodality.

- Report sampling time and NFE for high-resolution datasets (CelebA-HQ, LSUN Church) and discuss how the speedup factor scales with resolution.

- Acknowledge and discuss recent acceleration methods (Consistency Models, DPM-Solver) and compare the proposed method's speed-quality trade-off with these approaches.

- Temper the claim that the Gaussian assumption is the 'fundamental' cause of slow sampling, and clarify the trilemma as an empirical observation rather than a theoretical impossibility.

comments.title: Missing evaluation results and metrics in Section 5
comments.quote: In this section, we evaluate our proposed denoising diffusion GAN for the image synthesis problem. We begin with briefly introducing the network architecture design, while additional implementation details are presented in Appendix C.
comments.explanation: The section is titled '5 EXPERIMENTS' and claims to evaluate the model, yet the provided text only describes the generator architecture. It contains no quantitative results, no comparisons with baselines, no tables or figures, and no discussion of the experimental setup (datasets, metrics, training details). A results section must present actual outcomes—such as FID, IS, recall, sampling time, or NFE—to support the paper's claims. The authors should either include the full experimental results here or rename the section to 'Network Architecture' and move the evaluation to a later section.

comments.title: Missing justification for Gaussian form in small-step limit
comments.quote: First, in the limit of infinitesimal step size  $\beta_t$ , the product in the Bayes' rule is dominated by  $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  and the reversal of the diffusion process takes an identical functional form as the forward process (Feller, 1949). Thus, when  $\beta_t$  is sufficiently small, since  $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$  is a Gaussian, the denoising distribution  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$  is also Gaussian, and the approximation used by current diffusion models can be accurate.
comments.explanation: The claim that the denoising distribution is Gaussian when $\beta_t$ is small relies on the product $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ being dominated by the Gaussian $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. However, $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ is the marginal data distribution at step $t-1$, which can be highly non-Gaussian. The argument that the product is 'dominated' by the forward kernel is not mathematically justified: for small $\beta_t$, $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ is a narrow Gaussian centered at $\sqrt{1-\beta_t}\mathbf{x}_{t-1}$, but $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ can vary arbitrarily. The cited reference (Feller, 1949) applies to the reversal of diffusion processes with infinitesimal steps, but that result typically assumes the forward process is a continuous-time diffusion with smooth coefficients, not a discrete-time Gaussian kernel with arbitrary marginal. The paper would benefit from a more rigorous justification or a reference to the specific theorem that guarantees Gaussianity of the reverse conditional in the small-step limit. Add a brief explanation or cite a standard result (e.g., from Anderson, 1982) that the reverse process has the same functional form as the forward process in the continuous-time limit.

comments.title: Second condition for Gaussian denoising distribution is imprecise
comments.quote: Second, if data marginal  $q(\mathbf{x}_t)$  is Gaussian, the denoising distribution  $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$  is also a Gaussian distribution.
comments.explanation: The claim that a Gaussian marginal $q(\mathbf{x}_t)$ implies a Gaussian denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is not generally true. From Bayes' rule, $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t) \propto q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1}) q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$. Even if $q(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is Gaussian, $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ can be non-Gaussian, and the product need not be Gaussian. The correct statement is that if both $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ and $q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ are Gaussian, then $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is Gaussian. The paper's phrasing is misleading. Rewrite as: 'Second, if the marginal $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$ is Gaussian, then the denoising distribution $q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is also Gaussian because the product of two Gaussians is Gaussian.' Alternatively, clarify that the condition refers to the joint distribution being Gaussian.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated causal claim about Gaussian assumption
comments.quote: In this paper, we argue that slow sampling in these models is fundamentally attributed to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step which is justified only for small step sizes.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'fundamentally attributed' asserts a causal relationship that the paper does not rigorously establish. Other factors—discretization error, the noise schedule, and model capacity—also contribute to the need for many steps. The paper never runs a controlled experiment that isolates the Gaussian assumption from these confounders. Rewrite as 'we hypothesize that a key factor contributing to slow sampling is the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step, which is justified only for small step sizes.' This tempers the claim to match the evidence provided.

comments.title: Speedup claim lacks statistical reporting
comments.quote: Through extensive evaluations, we show that denoising diffusion GANs obtain sample quality and diversity competitive with original diffusion models while being  $2000 \times$  faster on the CIFAR-10 dataset.
comments.explanation: The 2000× speedup is reported as a single number with no indication of variability across runs. GAN training is sensitive to random seeds, and without standard deviations or confidence intervals, the reader cannot assess the reliability of this claim. The paper should report the mean and standard deviation over at least 3–5 independent runs, and ideally include a statistical test comparing to baselines. Add a sentence such as 'Over 5 independent runs, the speedup factor was 2000× ± 150× (mean ± std).'

comments.title: Speedup not verified on high-resolution datasets
comments.quote: Through extensive evaluations, we show that denoising diffusion GANs obtain sample quality and diversity competitive with original diffusion models while being  $2000 \times$  faster on the CIFAR-10 dataset.
comments.explanation: The 2000× speedup is only reported for CIFAR-10 (32×32). The paper also evaluates on CelebA-HQ and LSUN Church at 256×256 but omits sampling time and number of function evaluations for those datasets. Without these numbers, it is unclear whether the speedup generalizes to higher resolutions, where the denoising distribution may be more complex and the GAN may require more steps or larger networks. The authors should report NFE and wall-clock time for the high-resolution experiments, or explicitly state that the speedup factor is dataset-dependent.

comments.title: Trilemma framing oversimplified and contradicted by own results
comments.quote: We call the challenge imposed by these requirements the generative learning trilemma, as the existing models often trade some of them for others.
comments.explanation: The abstract presents the trilemma as a universal trade-off, yet the paper's own results show that the proposed model achieves competitive quality, coverage, and speed simultaneously—effectively 'solving' the trilemma. This contradiction suggests the trilemma is an empirical observation about existing methods, not a fundamental impossibility. The paper should clarify that the trilemma describes current limitations, not a theoretical bound, and discuss whether the proposed model truly overcomes it or merely shifts the trade-off (e.g., to training time or memory).

comments.title: Missing recall values for several diffusion models in Table 1
comments.quote: In contrast, our model obtains a significantly better recall score, even higher than several advanced likelihood-based models, and competitive among diffusion models.
comments.explanation: The claim that the proposed model's recall is 'competitive among diffusion models' is not fully supported by Table 1 because recall values are missing for many diffusion models listed (e.g., NCSN, Adversarial DSM, Likelihood SDE, Improved DDPM, VDM, UDM, D3PMs, Gotta Go Fast, DDPM Distillation). Without these values, it is impossible to assess how the proposed model's recall of 0.57 compares to the full set of diffusion baselines. The authors should either report recall for all baselines or restrict the comparison to models with available recall scores. This omission weakens the claim about diversity.

comments.title: Contradiction in recall values for T=4 vs T=2
comments.quote: When varying  $T > 1$ , we observe that  $T = 4$  gives the best results, whereas there is a slight degrade in performance for larger  $T$ .
comments.explanation: The text claims T=4 gives the best results, but Table 2 shows T=2 has a higher Inception Score (9.80 vs 9.63) and a lower FID (4.08 vs 3.75). While FID is lower for T=4, IS is higher for T=2, and the recall values are comparable (0.54 vs 0.57). The claim that T=4 is unambiguously best is not supported by the data. The authors should either revise the statement to acknowledge the trade-off or provide a justification for why FID is prioritized over IS in this comparison.

comments.title: Speedup claim for stroke-based synthesis is unverifiable
comments.quote: ed to Meng et al. (2021b), our model enjoys a  $1100 \times$  speedup in generation, as it takes only 0.16s to generate one image at 256 resolution vs. 181s for Meng et al. (2021b).
comments.explanation: The 1100× speedup claim is presented without any specification of hardware, batch size, or number of function evaluations (NFE) for either method. The 181s figure for Meng et al. (2021b) is not sourced in this section — it is unclear whether this is the authors' own measurement or a number taken from the cited paper. Without reporting the NFE for both models and the hardware configuration, the reader cannot assess whether the speedup is due to the proposed method's architecture or simply to differences in implementation (e.g., GPU model, software framework). The authors should state the NFE for both methods and the hardware used, or remove the wall-clock comparison and report only NFE.

comments.title: Missing sampling time and NFE for high-resolution datasets
comments.quote: lution Images: We train our model on datasets with larger images, including CelebAHQ (Karras et al., 2018) and LSUN Church (Yu et al., 2015) at  $256 \times 256$ px resolution. We report FID on these two datasets in Table 4 and 5. Similar to CIFAR-10, our model obtains competitive sample quality among the best diffusion models and GANs. In pa
comments.explanation: The paper's central claim is fast sampling, yet this section reports only FID for the 256×256 experiments and omits the number of sampling steps (NFE) and wall-clock time. Without these numbers, the reader cannot determine whether the speedup generalizes beyond CIFAR-10. The authors should add a column for NFE and sampling time to Tables 4 and 5, or at minimum state the NFE used for these datasets in the text.

comments.title: Overstated claim about overcoming the trilemma in conclusion
comments.quote: Our denoising diffusion GAN overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent, allowing diffusion models to be applied to real-world problems with low computational cost.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that the proposed model 'overcomes the generative learning trilemma to a large extent,' but the paper's own results show trade-offs remain. On CIFAR-10, the model achieves FID 3.75 with 4 steps, which is competitive but not state-of-the-art in quality (e.g., StyleGAN2 achieves FID 2.32). On higher-resolution datasets like LSUN Church, the FID is 5.25, which is worse than StyleGAN2's 3.86. The model also does not match the mode coverage of original diffusion models (recall 0.53 vs. 0.60 on CIFAR-10). Thus, the claim overstates the extent to which the trilemma is resolved. Rewrite the sentence as: 'Our denoising diffusion GAN substantially advances toward resolving the generative learning trilemma, achieving competitive quality and diversity with orders-of-magnitude faster sampling, though some trade-offs remain on high-resolution datasets.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about reducing negative social impacts
comments.quote: Thus, we foresee that in the long term, our model can help with reducing the negative social impacts of existing generative models that fall short in capturing the data diversity.
comments.explanation: This claim is speculative and not supported by any evidence in the paper. The paper does not demonstrate that the proposed model actually improves representation of minorities or reduces biases compared to other generative models. The evaluation is limited to standard image datasets (CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, LSUN Church) with standard metrics (FID, IS, recall), none of which measure fairness, bias, or representation of minorities. Remove this sentence or replace it with a concrete discussion of limitations and potential negative societal impacts, as required by many ethics guidelines.

comments.title: Reproducibility statement lacks concrete commitment
comments.quote: To aid with reproducibility further, we will release our source code publicly in the future with instructions to reproduce our results.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'in the future' is vague and does not constitute a firm reproducibility commitment. Top-tier venues typically require code release at the time of publication or at least a clear timeline (e.g., 'upon acceptance'). The authors should either commit to releasing code alongside the paper or provide a specific reason why code cannot be released (e.g., proprietary dependencies). Without code, many experimental details (e.g., exact GAN training procedure, random seeds, data preprocessing) may be insufficient for exact reproduction.

comments.title: Missing closing parenthesis in discriminator loss
comments.quote:
criminator is trained by:

$\min_{\phi}\sum_{t\geq 1}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t})}\big{[}\mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t)]+\mathbb{E}_{p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t})}[-\log(1-D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]\big{]},$ (5)

where fake samples
comments.explanation: In the first term inside the expectation, the expression `[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t)]` is missing a closing parenthesis after `t`. It should be `[-\log(D_{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\mathbf{x}_{t},t))]` to match the second term and standard notation. This is a typographical error that could cause confusion if the equation is read literally. Add the missing closing parenthesis.

comments.title: Forward reference of z in Equation (6)
comments.quote: $p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t}):=\int p_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{0}|\mathbf{x}_{t})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0})d\mathbf{x}_{0}=\int p(\mathbf{z})q(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{x}_{0}=G_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{z},t))d\mathbf{z},$
comments.explanation: The notation `p(z)` is introduced in the second integral of Equation (6) but `z` is not defined until the next sentence. The text immediately following says 'where ... $\mathbf{z}\sim p(\mathbf{z}):=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z};\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$', which defines `z` after its first use. This is a forward reference that could confuse readers. Move the definition of `z` before or into the equation, e.g., by writing 'where $\mathbf{z}\sim p(\mathbf{z}):=\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z};\mathbf{0},\mathbf{I})$' before the second integral.

comments.title: Typo in Jensen-Shannon divergence
comments.quote: where $D_{\mathrm{adv}}$ can be Wasserstein distance, Jenson-Shannon divergence, or f-divergence depending on the adversarial training setup
comments.explanation: The text spells 'Jenson-Shannon divergence' but the standard spelling is 'Jensen-Shannon divergence' (after Johan Jensen and Claude Shannon). This is a minor typographical error that should be corrected for consistency with the literature. Rewrite 'Jenson-Shannon' as 'Jensen-Shannon'.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a novel approach to accelerate diffusion models by modeling the denoising distribution with a GAN, allowing for large denoising steps. The empirical results on CIFAR-10 and higher-resolution datasets are strong, demonstrating competitive quality with substantial speedups. However, the theoretical framing of the speedup requires refinement, the comparison to distillation methods is empirically weak, and the claim of being the first model of this type is factually risky.

The work addresses a significant practical bottleneck in diffusion models by introducing a hybrid GAN-diffusion architecture. The empirical evidence supports the claim of solving the generative learning trilemma for image synthesis, particularly regarding mode coverage. The methodology is technically sound, though the theoretical justification for why this specific GAN setup yields better mode coverage than standard GANs is underdeveloped.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Framing of Slow Sampling: The paper attributes slow sampling *solely* to the Gaussian assumption in the denoising step (Section 2, 3.1). While it is true that the Gaussian approximation fails for large steps, this is a consequence of SDE discretization error rather than the direct cause of the slowness. The slowness exists because one must take small steps to keep the discretization error small enough for the Gaussian approximation to be valid. By framing the Gaussian assumption as the *fundamental* cause, the paper risks misrepresenting the underlying mathematics of diffusion models. The authors should clarify that the limitation is the discretization error of the SDE, which necessitates the Gaussian approximation, rather than the assumption itself.

- Weak Comparison to Distillation Methods: Section 4 claims that distillation methods like FastDDPM and DDPM Distillation 'suffer from significant degradation in sample quality.' However, Table 1 shows FastDDPM (T=50) with an FID of 3.41, while the proposed method (T=4) achieves an FID of 3.75. This represents a negligible degradation in quality for a 12.5x speedup. The claim of 'significant degradation' is empirically weak and undermines the paper's argument that distillation is a poor alternative. The authors should revise this comparison to accurately reflect the data, perhaps noting that while distillation is competitive, their method achieves similar quality with even fewer steps.

- Missing Theoretical Justification for Mode Coverage: The paper claims that their model exhibits 'better mode coverage and sample diversity' compared to traditional GANs (Section 5.1). They attribute this to the conditioning on $x_t$ and the smoothing effect of the diffusion process. However, the training objective minimizes Reverse KL divergence (a mode-seeking divergence), which typically causes mode collapse. There is no theoretical discussion on why minimizing Reverse KL on a *multimodal* posterior (the denoising distribution) leads to better mode coverage than minimizing it on the *unimodal* data distribution. This gap weakens the theoretical contribution, as it does not explain the mechanism by which the GAN overcomes its inherent bias.

- Factual Risk of 'First Model' Claim: The abstract states, 'To the best of our knowledge, denoising diffusion GAN is the first model that reduces sampling cost...'. This is a strong factual claim. The provided literature context (external search) lists 'SPI-GAN' (2023) and 'Latent Denoising Diffusion GAN' (2024), and the paper text cites 'Diffusion-GAN' (2022) in the related work (though the citation list in the provided text is incomplete). Even if the provided text body does not explicitly cite these competitors, the authors must rigorously verify this claim against the full timeline of arXiv papers to ensure it is not factually incorrect. A paper claiming to be 'first' requires exhaustive bibliographic verification.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The paper presents a compelling empirical contribution with a novel architectural idea. However, the theoretical framing of the speedup is slightly imprecise, the comparison to distillation is weak, and the claim of being the first model is factually risky. These issues prevent the paper from being accepted in its current form but are fixable with targeted revisions.

Revision targets:

- Clarify the theoretical link between step size, SDE discretization error, and the Gaussian assumption.

- Re-evaluate the comparison to distillation methods (FastDDPM, DDPM Distillation) to remove the 'significant degradation' claim if the data does not support it.

- Add a theoretical discussion on the bias of Reverse KL divergence and why it succeeds in covering modes for the multimodal posterior.

- Verify the 'first model' claim against the full literature and either remove it or provide a rigorous defense.

comments.title: Incorrect description of Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b)
comments.quote: Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) add an auxiliary adversarial loss to the main objective.
comments.explanation: The description of Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. (2021b) is factually incorrect. Their work, 'Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models' (DDIM), introduces a deterministic sampler and does not rely on an auxiliary adversarial loss. Please verify the citation and the description of the prior work.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Song et al. (2021a)
comments.quote: t al. (2021a) uses  $\mathbf{x}_0$  sampling as a crucial ingredient to their method, but their denoising distribution is still a Gaussian. These m
comments.explanation: The citation in this sentence is incorrect. Song et al. (2021a) refers to 'Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models' (DDIM), which is a deterministic sampler and does not rely on $x_0$ sampling as a crucial ingredient. If the authors intended to refer to a different method, please clarify the citation.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of ImageBART architecture
comments.quote: ImageBART (Esser et al., 2021a) explores modeling the denoising distribution of a diffusion process on discrete latent space with an auto-regressive model per step in a few denoising steps.
comments.explanation: The description of ImageBART is misleading. It is a text-to-image model operating on discrete tokens via VQ-VAE, not an auto-regressive model modeling the denoising distribution per step. Please revise this to accurately reflect the method.
